Extremely cold wind chills were observed over all of eastern Nebraska and southwest Iowa on the heels of the prolonged winter storm that dropped between 6 and 15 inches of snow across most of the region.  North winds of 20 to 30 mph with higher gusts late Monday slowly subsided into Tuesday morning, 1/27/04.  However, as temperatures fell into the single digits and then below zero, wind chill values of 20 below to 30 below were common throughout the region from around Midnight through noon on Tuesday.  Most actual lows over the area by Tuesday morning were 5 below to 15 below zero.  Slowly moderating temperatures and a somewhat decreased wind allowed some recovery in wind chill values toward noon Tuesday.

Although periods of moderate snow fell across far southeast Nebraska and extreme southwest Iowa during the afternoon of the 3rd, the steadier, more widespread and accumulating snow moved into the region during the late evening hours on the 3rd.  The snow continued into the mid afternoon hours on the 4th, shrinking to a band along Interstate-80 from Lincoln to Omaha before finally ending.Snowfall of between 5 and 6 inches fell across the entire area, with some amounts a little higher.  The higher totals included 8 inches in Oakland and Glenwood Iowa and 7 inches in La Platte, Gretna and the NWS in Valley, all in Nebraska.

The second night in a row of extremely cold wind chill values were observed across eastern Nebraska and southwest Iowa as a another surge of Arctic air spilled south across the region.  Temperatures by midnight on the 28th fell to around zero or below as north winds increased into the 10 to 20 mph range. The combination of the cold temperatures and brisk winds produced wind chill values of 15 below to 30 below zero over much of the area from midnight through noon on the 28th.

Bitterly cold temperatures followed a winter storm that struck parts of southeast Nebraska and southwest Iowa a few days prior.  Temperatures fell to zero or a little colder by 700 pm on the 5th and bottomed out between 5 below and 10 below zero over much of the region during the early morning hours on the 6th. When combined with westerly winds around 10 to 15 mph, wind chill values of 20 below to 25 below were common throughout the region from around 700 pm on the 5th through 1000 am on the 6th.The extreme cold was responsible for the death of a 72-year-old Omaha woman who died of hypothermia on her own porch.  The lightly dressed woman apparently went out to check on her car sometime after midnight and slipped on her way back into the house, possibly incapacitating her.

Two different low pressure systems moving across the plains brought a prolonged winter storm event to much of eastern Nebraska and southwest Iowa, dropping as much as 15 inches of snow by the time the storm ended.  The first system that moved out of the southwest U.S. brought moisture from the southern plains into the region resulting in freezing rain and sleet over sections of eastern Nebraska and southwest Iowa early on Sunday the 25th.  Some ice accumulations of 1/4 to 1/2 inch were reported from Jefferson county east through Gage county and north into Butler county.  The icing changed to snow and quickly spread northeast Sunday morning covering much of the region.  A few heavy snow bands set up from southeast Nebraska northeast into western Douglas county Nebraska and Pottawattamie county Iowa Sunday afternoon.The second low pressure system moved out of the northern Rockies Sunday night bringing Arctic air from Canada with it.  The combination of Arctic air and left over moisture from the previous system resulted in heavy snowfall rates across most of the region much of Sunday night into Monday the 26th.  The cold air was ushered in on north winds that gusted over 30 mph causing significant blowing and drifting snow Monday afternoon into Monday night.  The strong winds and fluffy snow caused large drifts and reduced visibilities.  As temperatures continued plunging Monday night, wind chills fell into the 20 below to 30 below zero range as actual temperatures fell below zero.  The storm caused the cancellation of most schools over the region Monday.  Many schools remained closed Tuesday due to a combination of the snow on the ground and the extremely cold wind chills that followed.Higher snowfall totals in eastern Nebraska included 15 inches at Elkhorn and Bellevue, 14 inches at Plattsmouth, 13 inches at Omaha Eppley, the NWS in Valley and in northeast Lincoln, with 12 inches reported at Lyons, Fremont, Uehling, Bennington, southwest Lincoln, Hickman, Raymond, Papillion, Springfield, Friend and Fort Calhoun.  Most other locations in eastern Nebraska received between 6 and 10 inches.Heavier totals in southwest Iowa included 14 inches at Glenwood, 13 inches in Oakland, 12 inches in Little Sioux, Harlan and Castana and 11 inches at Mapleton and Underwood.  Most other locations in southwest Iowa received 6 to 10 inches.

Most actual lows over the area by Tuesday morning were 5 below to 15 below zero.  Slowly moderating temperatures and a somewhat decreased wind allowed some recovery in wind chill values toward noon Tuesday.

Bitterly cold temperatures followed a winter storm that struck parts of southeast Nebraska and southwest Iowa a few days prior.  Temperatures fell to zero or a little colder by 700 pm on the 5th and bottomed out between 5 below and 10 below zero over much of the region during the early morning hours on the 6th. When combined with westerly winds around 10 to 15 mph, wind chill values of 20 below to 25 below were common throughout the region from around 700 pm on the 5th through 1000 am on the 6th.The extreme cold was responsible for the death of a 72-year-old Omaha woman who died of hypothermia on her own porch.  The lightly dressed woman apparently went out to check on her car sometime after midnight and slipped on her way back into the house, possibly incapacitating her.

Although periods of moderate snow fell across far southeast Nebraska and extreme southwest Iowa during the afternoon of the 3rd, the steadier, more widespread and accumulating snow moved into the region during the late evening hours on the 3rd.  The snow continued into the mid afternoon hours on the 4th, shrinking to a band along Interstate-80 from Lincoln to Omaha before finally ending.Snowfall of between 5 and 6 inches fell across the entire area, with some amounts a little higher.  The higher totals included 8 inches in Oakland and Glenwood Iowa and 7 inches in La Platte, Gretna and the NWS in Valley, all in Nebraska.

The second night in a row of extremely cold wind chill values were observed across eastern Nebraska and southwest Iowa as a another surge of Arctic air spilled south across the region.  Temperatures by midnight on the 28th fell to around zero or below as north winds increased into the 10 to 20 mph range. The combination of the cold temperatures and brisk winds produced wind chill values of 15 below to 30 below zero over much of the area from midnight through noon on the 28th.

An upper level trough moved eastward out of the Rockies into the High Plains of Nebraska and with abundant moisture created a  favorable situation for moderate to occasionally heavy snow over the southern counties of western Nebraska. Along with the strong low level winds, snow...drifting and blowing...created hazardous conditions for travelers. Snowfall totals for this event were 8 inches at Lamar in Chase County...6 to 8 inches at Curtis in Frontier County and 6 inches at Hayes Center in Hayes County. Numerous secondary roads were closed due to snow drifts and blowing snow. At least one non-injury accident occurred in Keith County in which weather conditions contributed.

When combined with westerly winds around 10 to 15 mph, wind chill values of 20 below to 25 below were common throughout the region from around 700 pm on the 5th through 1000 am on the 6th.The extreme cold was responsible for the death of a 72-year-old Omaha woman who died of hypothermia on her own porch.  The lightly dressed woman apparently went out to check on her car sometime after midnight and slipped on her way back into the house, possibly incapacitating her.

Snowfall of 6 to 12 inches was accompanied by winds strong enough to reduce visibilities to a quarter mile or less at times in blowing snow. The winds also caused drifting snow, which contributed to making travel very difficult. In addition, dangerous wind chills developed late in the storm as actual temperatures fell. Most schools were closed and several school related activities were cancelled.

The second night in a row of extremely cold wind chill values were observed across eastern Nebraska and southwest Iowa as a another surge of Arctic air spilled south across the region.  Temperatures by midnight on the 28th fell to around zero or below as north winds increased into the 10 to 20 mph range. The combination of the cold temperatures and brisk winds produced wind chill values of 15 below to 30 below zero over much of the area from midnight through noon on the 28th.

Extremely cold wind chills were observed over all of eastern Nebraska and southwest Iowa on the heels of the prolonged winter storm that dropped between 6 and 15 inches of snow across most of the region.  North winds of 20 to 30 mph with higher gusts late Monday slowly subsided into Tuesday morning, 1/27/04.  However, as temperatures fell into the single digits and then below zero, wind chill values of 20 below to 30 below were common throughout the region from around Midnight through noon on Tuesday.  Most actual lows over the area by Tuesday morning were 5 below to 15 below zero.  Slowly moderating temperatures and a somewhat decreased wind allowed some recovery in wind chill values toward noon Tuesday.

Two different low pressure systems moving across the plains brought a prolonged winter storm event to much of eastern Nebraska and southwest Iowa, dropping as much as 15 inches of snow by the time the storm ended.  The first system that moved out of the southwest U.S. brought moisture from the southern plains into the region resulting in freezing rain and sleet over sections of eastern Nebraska and southwest Iowa early on Sunday the 25th.  Some ice accumulations of 1/4 to 1/2 inch were reported from Jefferson county east through Gage county and north into Butler county.  The icing changed to snow and quickly spread northeast Sunday morning covering much of the region.  A few heavy snow bands set up from southeast Nebraska northeast into western Douglas county Nebraska and Pottawattamie county Iowa Sunday afternoon.The second low pressure system moved out of the northern Rockies Sunday night bringing Arctic air from Canada with it.  The combination of Arctic air and left over moisture from the previous system resulted in heavy snowfall rates across most of the region much of Sunday night into Monday the 26th.  The cold air was ushered in on north winds that gusted over 30 mph causing significant blowing and drifting snow Monday afternoon into Monday night.  The strong winds and fluffy snow caused large drifts and reduced visibilities.  As temperatures continued plunging Monday night, wind chills fell into the 20 below to 30 below zero range as actual temperatures fell below zero.  The storm caused the cancellation of most schools over the region Monday.  Many schools remained closed Tuesday due to a combination of the snow on the ground and the extremely cold wind chills that followed.Higher snowfall totals in eastern Nebraska included 15 inches at Elkhorn and Bellevue, 14 inches at Plattsmouth, 13 inches at Omaha Eppley, the NWS in Valley and in northeast Lincoln, with 12 inches reported at Lyons, Fremont, Uehling, Bennington, southwest Lincoln, Hickman, Raymond, Papillion, Springfield, Friend and Fort Calhoun.  Most other locations in eastern Nebraska received between 6 and 10 inches.Heavier totals in southwest Iowa included 14 inches at Glenwood, 13 inches in Oakland, 12 inches in Little Sioux, Harlan and Castana and 11 inches at Mapleton and Underwood.  Most other locations in southwest Iowa received 6 to 10 inches.

Most other locations in eastern Nebraska received between 6 and 10 inches.Heavier totals in southwest Iowa included 14 inches at Glenwood, 13 inches in Oakland, 12 inches in Little Sioux, Harlan and Castana and 11 inches at Mapleton and Underwood.  Most other locations in southwest Iowa received 6 to 10 inches.

Two different low pressure systems moving across the plains brought a prolonged winter storm event to much of eastern Nebraska and southwest Iowa, dropping as much as 15 inches of snow by the time the storm ended.  The first system that moved out of the southwest U.S. brought moisture from the southern plains into the region resulting in freezing rain and sleet over sections of eastern Nebraska and southwest Iowa early on Sunday the 25th.  Some ice accumulations of 1/4 to 1/2 inch were reported from Jefferson county east through Gage county and north into Butler county.  The icing changed to snow and quickly spread northeast Sunday morning covering much of the region.  A few heavy snow bands set up from southeast Nebraska northeast into western Douglas county Nebraska and Pottawattamie county Iowa Sunday afternoon.The second low pressure system moved out of the northern Rockies Sunday night bringing Arctic air from Canada with it.  The combination of Arctic air and left over moisture from the previous system resulted in heavy snowfall rates across most of the region much of Sunday night into Monday the 26th.  The cold air was ushered in on north winds that gusted over 30 mph causing significant blowing and drifting snow Monday afternoon into Monday night.  The strong winds and fluffy snow caused large drifts and reduced visibilities.  As temperatures continued plunging Monday night, wind chills fell into the 20 below to 30 below zero range as actual temperatures fell below zero.  The storm caused the cancellation of most schools over the region Monday.  Many schools remained closed Tuesday due to a combination of the snow on the ground and the extremely cold wind chills that followed.Higher snowfall totals in eastern Nebraska included 15 inches at Elkhorn and Bellevue, 14 inches at Plattsmouth, 13 inches at Omaha Eppley, the NWS in Valley and in northeast Lincoln, with 12 inches reported at Lyons, Fremont, Uehling, Bennington, southwest Lincoln, Hickman, Raymond, Papillion, Springfield, Friend and Fort Calhoun.  Most other locations in eastern Nebraska received between 6 and 10 inches.Heavier totals in southwest Iowa included 14 inches at Glenwood, 13 inches in Oakland, 12 inches in Little Sioux, Harlan and Castana and 11 inches at Mapleton and Underwood.  Most other locations in southwest Iowa received 6 to 10 inches.

A winter storm pounded a section of south-central Nebraska with heavy snow and strong winds.  The storm primarily blasted areas along and east of U. S. Highway 281 from late Sunday evening into Monday.Snow began in earnest by late Sunday afternoon, and turned heavy at times overnight.  The snow lingered across eastern sections of the area on Monday as the winds increased and temperatures dropped.  The heaviest snow fell across York county where 12 inches of new snow was reported.  Up to 10 inches were measured in Shickley and Nelson and 6 to 8 inches in the Grand Island area.  The falling snow tapered off, but the blowing snow began. Snow, being whipped by 35 mph north winds, caused travel to become very treacherous.  Many schools canceled classes as rural roads were almost impassable on the 26th.  Temperatures took a nose dive and fell below zero over nearly the entire area the night of January 26th.There were numerous accidents around the region, including over 40 in York county alone.  In one accident near the town of York, the driver of a SUV lost control and hit a truck.  A 5 year old girl riding in the SUV was thrown from the vehicle and died from her injuries.

After a balmy start to the year with temperatures in the 60s on New Year's Day, the region quickly plummeted into winter with the first widespread snowfall.  The snow fell over the course of about 24 hours and in two separate bursts.  The first snow swath fell from north of Minden to about York by midday on the 3rd.  Snow then redeveloped by evening and continued overnight before ending by noon on the 4th.  A wide swath of 6 to 8 inches fell along and either side of Interstate 80.  Trumbull reported 9 inches of snow, Hastings about 8 inches, and Henderson 6 to 8 inches.In the case of areas west of Holdrege, this snow was the first significant precipitation since early October.  Specifically, Oxford had received precipitation on only 8 days since October 1st and just a trace during the month of December.  So the 6 inches of fresh snow was certainly a welcome site in Oxford and other areas of drought stricken south-central Nebraska.Once the snow had stopped, the coldest air of the season settled across the area.  Temperatures fell well below zero by the 6th.  Minden dropped to 19 degrees below zero and Lexington bottomed out at 26 degrees below zero.

Up to 10 inches were measured in Shickley and Nelson and 6 to 8 inches in the Grand Island area.  The falling snow tapered off, but the blowing snow began. Snow, being whipped by 35 mph north winds, caused travel to become very treacherous.  Many schools canceled classes as rural roads were almost impassable on the 26th.  Temperatures took a nose dive and fell below zero over nearly the entire area the night of January 26th.There were numerous accidents around the region, including over 40 in York county alone.  In one accident near the town of York, the driver of a SUV lost control and hit a truck.  A 5 year old girl riding in the SUV was thrown from the vehicle and died from her injuries.

This was the third winter storm that hit most of eastern Nebraska and southwest Iowa in a week and a half period.  Light to occasionally moderate snow began over the western sections of the area Wednesday afternoon or evening 2/4/04.  The snow intensity and coverage then increased eastward into Thursday morning.  From late Wednesday through 6am Thursday, locations from around Fairbury north northwest through Albion and Loretto picked up 4 to 6 inches of snow.  Locations farther east received much less.  However, the heavier snow soon spread over the rest of the area after 6 am.  The snow decreased to flurries or ended over southeast Nebraska around noon but continued into late afternoon to the northwest through northeast of Fremont and Omaha.  Although snow continued into Friday 2/6/04 roughly along and northeast of a line from around Columbus to Omaha, additional accumulations after late afternoon on the 5th were mostly an inch or so.  For the storm through Thursday afternoon most locations reported between 5 and 8 inches.  This storm again caused many schools to close, some as early as Thursday afternoon, but many on Friday.Some of the heavier storm totals in eastern Nebraska included...10 inches at Loretto and Albion, 9 inches in Springfield, 8 inches in Elkhorn, Bennington, Norfolk, Gretna, Papillion and Walthill and 7 inches at West Point, Fremont, Uehling, the NWS in Valley and at the Lincoln airport.  Omaha Eppley picked up 6.4 inches, and when combined with the two previous storms, the total snow depth reached 26 inches. This was just one inch short of the all time record in Omaha.Some heavier storm totals in western Iowa included...8 inches in Harlan, 7 inches at Little Sioux, Underwood and Oakland.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Reported near 72nd and Grover Streets.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A slow moving storm system moved across Nebraska providing a prolonged snow event across portions of southwestern and central Nebraska. Snow amounts of up to 9 inches were reported in Custer County with 6 inch amounts reported across portions of southwestern Nebraska. Many schools were closed due to secondary roads closing from drifting snow. On Wednesday morning Feb 4, 2 fatalities (indirect) were reported due to a car accident on Interstate 80. A couple from Chadron, NE were killed when they lost control of their vehicle, crossed the median, moved into oncoming traffic and was struck by a semi.

The second winter storm in a week hit most of eastern Nebraska and southwest Iowa.  The first piece of energy from this storm affected mainly extreme northeast Nebraska dropping between 5 and 7 inches of snow over the northern half of Cedar and Knox counties.  The snow increased in intensity over far northeast Nebraska Saturday evening the 31st and decreased then by Sunday morning 2/1/04.  Although some additional light snow fell over the area on Sunday, most of the accumulating snow from this storm fell from late Saturday into early Sunday.  The heaviest snow from this storm fell south of the region on Sunday.

The second winter storm in a week hit a large part of southeast and east central Nebraska and parts of southwest Iowa.  Precipitation began over much the area late Saturday evening the 31st as Freezing drizzle.  The freezing drizzle changed to snow by early Sunday February 1st and continued into Monday morning the 2nd.  During a 2 to 3 hour period Sunday afternoon a heavy snow band set up from around Beatrice northeast through eastern Lincoln, into Omaha and then toward Harlan Iowa.  The band was only 10 to 20 miles wide, but snowfall rates under the heaviest part of it were on the order of 2 inches or even more per hour.  The band lifted slowly northeast across nearly the same area from around noon through 3pm before weakening and merging with the rest of the snow that was occurring across the region at that time.  Thus, those locations under the band reported the greatest storm totals with this storm. The storm closed many schools for the second Monday in a row, and some schools remained closed Tuesday, especially those that were under the heavier snow band.Omaha Eppley picked up 11 inches of snow from this storm.  When combined with the storm from the previous week, the snow depth reached 21 inches as of 900 am on 2/2/04.  That set a record snow depth for the month of February.  Most other locations throughout the Omaha area reported 8 to 10 inches of snowfall.  The snow accumulations from the two storms caused a few roofs of weaker structures to collapse, including the roof of a barn near Arlington which killed a number of livestock.Additional higher amounts from the storm in eastern Nebraska included 10 inches at Gretna and Papillion, 9 inches at Tecumseh, and eastern Lincoln, and 7 to 8 inches at Elkhorn, the NWS in Valley, Beatrice, Hickman, Pawnee City, Springfield, Wilber, and Tobias.Higher storm totals in western Iowa included 9 inches at Logan, 8 inches at Oakland and Harlan and 7 inches in Little Sioux, Glenwood, Pacific Junction, Castana, and Underwood.

Precipitation began over much the area late Saturday evening the 31st as Freezing drizzle.  The freezing drizzle changed to snow by early Sunday February 1st and continued into Monday morning the 2nd.  During a 2 to 3 hour period Sunday afternoon a heavy snow band set up from around Beatrice northeast through eastern Lincoln, into Omaha and then toward Harlan Iowa.  The band was only 10 to 20 miles wide, but snowfall rates under the heaviest part of it were on the order of 2 inches or even more per hour.  The band lifted slowly northeast across nearly the same area from around noon through 3pm before weakening and merging with the rest of the snow that was occurring across the region at that time.  Thus, those locations under the band reported the greatest storm totals with this storm. The storm closed many schools for the second Monday in a row, and some schools remained closed Tuesday, especially those that were under the heavier snow band.Omaha Eppley picked up 11 inches of snow from this storm.  When combined with the storm from the previous week, the snow depth reached 21 inches as of 900 am on 2/2/04.  That set a record snow depth for the month of February.  Most other locations throughout the Omaha area reported 8 to 10 inches of snowfall. This was the third winter storm that hit most of eastern Nebraska and southwest Iowa in a week and a half period.  Light to occasionally moderate snow began over the western sections of the area Wednesday afternoon or evening 2/4/04.  The snow intensity and coverage then increased eastward into Thursday morning.  From late Wednesday through 6am Thursday, locations from around Fairbury north northwest through Albion and Loretto picked up 4 to 6 inches of snow.  Locations farther east received much less.  However, the heavier snow soon spread over the rest of the area after 6 am.  The snow decreased to flurries or ended over southeast Nebraska around noon but continued into late afternoon to the northwest through northeast of Fremont and Omaha.  Although snow continued into Friday 2/6/04 roughly along and northeast of a line from around Columbus to Omaha, additional accumulations after late afternoon on the 5th were mostly an inch or so.  For the storm through Thursday afternoon most locations reported between 5 and 8 inches.  This storm again caused many schools to close, some as early as Thursday afternoon, but many on Friday.Some of the heavier storm totals in eastern Nebraska included...10 inches at Loretto and Albion, 9 inches in Springfield, 8 inches in Elkhorn, Bennington, Norfolk, Gretna, Papillion and Walthill and 7 inches at West Point, Fremont, Uehling, the NWS in Valley and at the Lincoln airport.  Omaha Eppley picked up 6.4 inches, and when combined with the two previous storms, the total snow depth reached 26 inches. This was just one inch short of the all time record in Omaha.Some heavier storm totals in western Iowa included...8 inches in Harlan, 7 inches at Little Sioux, Underwood and Oakland.

A slow moving storm system moved across Nebraska providing a prolonged snow event across portions of southwestern and central Nebraska. Snow amounts of up to 9 inches were reported in Custer County with 6 inch amounts reported across portions of southwestern Nebraska. Many schools were closed due to secondary roads closing from drifting snow. On Wednesday morning Feb 4, 2 fatalities (indirect) were reported due to a car accident on Interstate 80. A couple from Chadron, NE were killed when they lost control of their vehicle, crossed the median, moved into oncoming traffic and was struck by a semi.

The second winter storm in a week hit most of eastern Nebraska and southwest Iowa.  The first piece of energy from this storm affected mainly extreme northeast Nebraska dropping between 5 and 7 inches of snow over the northern half of Cedar and Knox counties.  The snow increased in intensity over far northeast Nebraska Saturday evening the 31st and decreased then by Sunday morning 2/1/04.  Although some additional light snow fell over the area on Sunday, most of the accumulating snow from this storm fell from late Saturday into early Sunday.  The heaviest snow from this storm fell south of the region on Sunday.

A major winter storm dumped heavy snow across most of south-central Nebraska.  The snow began early on the 4th and continued through about dawn on the 5th.  Heavy snow fell in two separate bursts, with the second burst producing snowfall rates of 1 to 2 inches per hour.  The heaviest snow fell along the U.S. Highway 281 corridor where 8 to 12 inches was common.  Specifically, Grand Island and Greeley checked in with 12 inches, Cairo recorded 11 inches and Hastings had 10 inches.  Farther east, Osceola measured 11 inches and Superior around 9 inches.  To the west, Kearney and Minden both had 6 to 7 inches of new snow.  Nearly every school in the region was closed on the 5th.  Fortunately, there was little wind initially with the snowfall and conditions were conducive to plowing roads and clearing sidewalks. There were a few accidents, especially along the interstate.  One man was killed after the Blazer, in which he was a passenger in, spun out of control on the icy roadway.   The snow also brought beneficial moisture to the region.

Strong northwest winds, gusting as high as 40 mph, combined with heavy snow cover to cause widespread blowing and drifting snow. Visibilities were frequently below a half mile in open areas. The drifting and low visibilities made travel hazardous, and slippery roads caused by the drifting added to the hazard. Numerous schools either cancelled classes or released students early. Light snow accumulated to less than a half inch, but added to the visibility reduction at times.

The heaviest snow fell along the U.S. Highway 281 corridor where 8 to 12 inches was common.  Specifically, Grand Island and Greeley checked in with 12 inches, Cairo recorded 11 inches and Hastings had 10 inches.  Farther east, Osceola measured 11 inches and Superior around 9 inches.  To the west, Kearney and Minden both had 6 to 7 inches of new snow.  Nearly every school in the region was closed on the 5th.  Fortunately, there was little wind initially with the snowfall and conditions were conducive to plowing roads and clearing sidewalks. There were a few accidents, especially along the interstate.  One man was killed after the Blazer, in which he was a passenger in, spun out of control on the icy roadway.   The snow also brought beneficial moisture to the region.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow and strong winds occurred over much of the Nebraska panhandle creating blizzard conditions.  Snowfall amounts from 6 to 10 inches were reported from many areas with amounts near 1 foot reported in the Chadron, NE. area.  Strong winds also created very low visibilities and drifts up to 3 feet with drifting snow resulting in many road closures.

Strong northwest winds, gusting as high as 40 mph, combined with heavy snow cover to cause widespread blowing and drifting snow. Visibilities were frequently below a half mile in open areas. The drifting and low visibilities made travel hazardous, and slippery roads caused by the drifting added to the hazard. Numerous schools either cancelled classes or released students early. Light snow accumulated to less than a half inch, but added to the visibility reduction at times.

A winter storm produced 2-4 inches of snow across extreme northwest Kansas, southwest Nebraska and eastern Colorado during the afternoon and overnight hours of February 29th. The snow was accompanied by a prolonged period of 40 to 60 mph winds which brought whiteout conditions and road closures.  Interstate 70 was closed for approximately 15 hours from Colby, Kansas to the Denver metro area. The highest wind gust with this storm was 66 mph reported at Flagler Colorado, with numerous reports of 45 to 55 mph winds elsewhere in northwest Kansas, southwest Nebraska and eastern Colorado.

Snowfall of 5 to 7 inches was preceded by freezing rain, leaving an icy layer beneath the snow on roads. Several vehicles slid off roads and into ditches. Winds gusting up to 30 mph caused drifting snow which made travel difficult. Classes were cancelled or delayed at several schools.

This was the third winter storm that hit most of eastern Nebraska and southwest Iowa in a week and a half period.  Light to occasionally moderate snow began over the western sections of the area Wednesday afternoon or evening 2/4/04.  The snow intensity and coverage then increased eastward into Thursday morning.  From late Wednesday through 6am Thursday, locations from around Fairbury north northwest through Albion and Loretto picked up 4 to 6 inches of snow.  Locations farther east received much less.  However, the heavier snow soon spread over the rest of the area after 6 am.  The snow decreased to flurries or ended over southeast Nebraska around noon but continued into late afternoon to the northwest through northeast of Fremont and Omaha.  Although snow continued into Friday 2/6/04 roughly along and northeast of a line from around Columbus to Omaha, additional accumulations after late afternoon on the 5th were mostly an inch or so.  For the storm through Thursday afternoon most locations reported between 5 and 8 inches.  This storm again caused many schools to close, some as early as Thursday afternoon, but many on Friday.Some of the heavier storm totals in eastern Nebraska included...10 inches at Loretto and Albion, 9 inches in Springfield, 8 inches in Elkhorn, Bennington, Norfolk, Gretna, Papillion and Walthill and 7 inches at West Point, Fremont, Uehling, the NWS in Valley and at the Lincoln airport.  Omaha Eppley picked up 6.4 inches, and when combined with the two previous storms, the total snow depth reached 26 inches. This was just one inch short of the all time record in Omaha.Some heavier storm totals in western Iowa included...8 inches in Harlan, 7 inches at Little Sioux, Underwood and Oakland.

A strong winter storm produced gusty winds, heavy snowfall, and icy conditions to much of western and north central Nebraska. Blizzard conditions were seen across the area as reports of up to 9 inches of snow occurred. This coupled with strong winds provided blinding blowing snow, which in return closed many highways, secondary roads, businesses and schools. Even I-80 in Deuel County was closed for a period of time as blizzard conditions quickly swept across the area.

A slow moving storm system moved across Nebraska providing a prolonged snow event across portions of southwestern and central Nebraska. Snow amounts of up to 9 inches were reported in Custer County with 6 inch amounts reported across portions of southwestern Nebraska. Many schools were closed due to secondary roads closing from drifting snow. On Wednesday morning Feb 4, 2 fatalities (indirect) were reported due to a car accident on Interstate 80. A couple from Chadron, NE were killed when they lost control of their vehicle, crossed the median, moved into oncoming traffic and was struck by a semi.

After a balmy start to the year with temperatures in the 60s on New Year's Day, the region quickly plummeted into winter with the first widespread snowfall.  The snow fell over the course of about 24 hours and in two separate bursts.  The first snow swath fell from north of Minden to about York by midday on the 3rd.  Snow then redeveloped by evening and continued overnight before ending by noon on the 4th.  A wide swath of 6 to 8 inches fell along and either side of Interstate 80.  Trumbull reported 9 inches of snow, Hastings about 8 inches, and Henderson 6 to 8 inches.In the case of areas west of Holdrege, this snow was the first significant precipitation since early October.  Specifically, Oxford had received precipitation on only 8 days since October 1st and just a trace during the month of December.  So the 6 inches of fresh snow was certainly a welcome site in Oxford and other areas of drought stricken south-central Nebraska.Once the snow had stopped, the coldest air of the season settled across the area.  Temperatures fell well below zero by the 6th.  Minden dropped to 19 degrees below zero and Lexington bottomed out at 26 degrees below zero.

The snow fell over the course of about 24 hours and in two separate bursts.  The first snow swath fell from north of Minden to about York by midday on the 3rd.  Snow then redeveloped by evening and continued overnight before ending by noon on the 4th.  A wide swath of 6 to 8 inches fell along and either side of Interstate 80.  Trumbull reported 9 inches of snow, Hastings about 8 inches, and Henderson 6 to 8 inches.In the case of areas west of Holdrege, this snow was the first significant precipitation since early October.  Specifically, Oxford had received precipitation on only 8 days since October 1st and just a trace during the month of December.  So the 6 inches of fresh snow was certainly a welcome site in Oxford and other areas of drought stricken south-central Nebraska.Once the snow had stopped, the coldest air of the season settled across the area.  Temperatures fell well below zero by the 6th.  Minden dropped to 19 degrees below zero and Lexington bottomed out at 26 degrees below zero.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A thunderstorm, which produced wind gusts that were measured at a little over 60 mph by home equipment at a Cooperative Observers residence just northeast of Gretna, eventually snapped the tops of some trees just west of Bellevue.

Thunderstorm wind gusts estimated at around 60 mph severely damaged a gazebo in the city park.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: This was the third winter storm that hit most of eastern Nebraska and southwest Iowa in a week and a half period.  Light to occasionally moderate snow began over the western sections of the area Wednesday afternoon or evening 2/4/04.  The snow intensity and coverage then increased eastward into Thursday morning.  From late Wednesday through 6am Thursday, locations from around Fairbury north northwest through Albion and Loretto picked up 4 to 6 inches of snow.  Locations farther east received much less.  However, the heavier snow soon spread over the rest of the area after 6 am.  The snow decreased to flurries or ended over southeast Nebraska around noon but continued into late afternoon to the northwest through northeast of Fremont and Omaha.  Although snow continued into Friday 2/6/04 roughly along and northeast of a line from around Columbus to Omaha, additional accumulations after late afternoon on the 5th were mostly an inch or so.  For the storm through Thursday afternoon most locations reported between 5 and 8 inches.  This storm again caused many schools to close, some as early as Thursday afternoon, but many on Friday.Some of the heavier storm totals in eastern Nebraska included...10 inches at Loretto and Albion, 9 inches in Springfield, 8 inches in Elkhorn, Bennington, Norfolk, Gretna, Papillion and Walthill and 7 inches at West Point, Fremont, Uehling, the NWS in Valley and at the Lincoln airport.  Omaha Eppley picked up 6.4 inches, and when combined with the two previous storms, the total snow depth reached 26 inches. This was just one inch short of the all time record in Omaha.Some heavier storm totals in western Iowa included...8 inches in Harlan, 7 inches at Little Sioux, Underwood and Oakland.

A late winter storm dropped heavy snow over parts of northeast Nebraska and western Iowa.  Due to relatively warm temperatures during the event, most of the snow was heavy and wet and the rain/snow line remained nearly stationary for much of that time. In addition...with temperatures a little above freezing on the southern end of the snow area, snow accumulations dropped off sharply.  For instance, a small area in far north Omaha picked up 5 to 6 inches, while the southern part of the city received only an inch or two, and then only on grassy surfaces.  Meanwhile 40 miles or so the southwest much of the snow melted as it fell leaving only a trace.  Most of the heavier snow, 6 inches or more, fell along and northeast of a line from around Center in Knox county to just east and northeast of Council Bluffs in Pottawattamie county in west central Iowa.Heavier amounts in eastern Nebraska included 13.5 inches in Crofton, 10 inches in Walthill and Oakland, 9 inches at Lyons, 8 inches at Wausa and Creighton, 7 inches at Fort Calhoun, Tekamah, West Point, and Blair.Heavier totals in western Iowa included around 13 inches at Castana and near Little Sioux, 11 inches in Mapleton, 9 inches at Harlan and 8 inches in Logan and Kennebec.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The first widespread area of thunderstorms of the season brought rain and hail to the region this Saturday afternoon.  The hail was scattered about south-central Nebraska generally west of U.S. Highway 281 and south of Interstate 80.  The hail ranged from pennies to quarters in size.  Rain amounts were in the one half to one inch range.

An intense low pressure area over the Northern Plains and a strong mid-level jetstream combined to produce a widespread high wind and blowing dirt event across eastern Colorado, western Kansas and parts of SW Nebraska.  Wind gusts of 50 to 65 mph were common from late morning through the afternoon hours causing reduced visibility in blowing dirt.  Many areas suffered minor tree and fence damage and zero visibility at times.  60 mph winds were reported in McCook in Red Willow county.

Rainfall of 2 to 3 inches fell across a large part of the Little Nemaha River basin Saturday evening and night on the 29th.  The heavy rain caused the Little Nemaha River to surpass its 22 foot flood stage near Auburn from 315 am Cdt through 830 am on May 30th.  The river crested at 23.1 feet around 500 am Cdt. The flooding affected mainly agricultural lowlands.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A rapidly deepening low pressure resulted in widespread winds of 40 to 60 mph across all of south central Nebraska. Wind gusts of 60 mph were reported at Kearney and Lexington, 58 mph at Holdrege, 56 mph at Grand Island and 55 mph at Hastings. Damage was sporadic over the region. A sign was ripped from a furniture store in Holdrege. In Grand Island, a light pole broke at an auto dealership and damaged four vehicles as it fell to the ground. Damage to the vehicles along was estimated at $14,000. There were numerous reports of tree damage and wind power line damage elsewhere in the area. The wind also kicked up dirt from freshly plowed fields. As a result, visibilities were down to 2 to 3 miles at times.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Heavy rains of at least 2 to 4 inches fell over much of southeast Nebraska and southwest Iowa from May 21st through the 24th.  The rains not only flooded smaller creeks and rivers over the area, but also pushed the Missouri River above flood stage from around Brownville through Rulo.  The river crested at Brownville at 34.1 feet at 5 pm Cdt on the 25th, flood stage is 32 feet.  The river at Rulo crested at 18.1 feet at 4pm Cdt on the 25th, flood stage is 17 feet. The flooding affected mainly agricultural lowlands.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cluster of decaying thunderstorms produced an area of downburst winds which spread southeast across Butler, Lancaster, Saunders and Saline counties.  The winds were measured at 69 mph by the ASOS at the Lincoln airport and at 62 mph by emergency management in Wilber. Gusts of similar magnitudes were reported west of Rising City, southwest of Weston and in De Witt.  However, these strong winds were likely widespread over much of each of these 4 counties.  The winds downed several trees in De Witt and did minor tree damage in Wilber.  The winds also caused areas of blowing dust.  The blowing dust reduced the visibility to zero near the junction of Highways 81 and 92 on the Butler and Polk county line causing an eight car pileup with 7 minor injuries.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A major winter storm dumped heavy snow across most of south-central Nebraska.  The snow began early on the 4th and continued through about dawn on the 5th.  Heavy snow fell in two separate bursts, with the second burst producing snowfall rates of 1 to 2 inches per hour.  The heaviest snow fell along the U.S. Highway 281 corridor where 8 to 12 inches was common.  Specifically, Grand Island and Greeley checked in with 12 inches, Cairo recorded 11 inches and Hastings had 10 inches.  Farther east, Osceola measured 11 inches and Superior around 9 inches.  To the west, Kearney and Minden both had 6 to 7 inches of new snow.  Nearly every school in the region was closed on the 5th.  Fortunately, there was little wind initially with the snowfall and conditions were conducive to plowing roads and clearing sidewalks. There were a few accidents, especially along the interstate.  One man was killed after the Blazer, in which he was a passenger in, spun out of control on the icy roadway.   The snow also brought beneficial moisture to the region.

To the west, Kearney and Minden both had 6 to 7 inches of new snow.  Nearly every school in the region was closed on the 5th.  Fortunately, there was little wind initially with the snowfall and conditions were conducive to plowing roads and clearing sidewalks. There were a few accidents, especially along the interstate.  One man was killed after the Blazer, in which he was a passenger in, spun out of control on the icy roadway.   The snow also brought beneficial moisture to the region.

A late winter storm dropped heavy snow over parts of northeast Nebraska and western Iowa.  Due to relatively warm temperatures during the event, most of the snow was heavy and wet and the rain/snow line remained nearly stationary for much of that time. In addition...with temperatures a little above freezing on the southern end of the snow area, snow accumulations dropped off sharply.  For instance, a small area in far north Omaha picked up 5 to 6 inches, while the southern part of the city received only an inch or two, and then only on grassy surfaces.  Meanwhile 40 miles or so the southwest much of the snow melted as it fell leaving only a trace.  Most of the heavier snow, 6 inches or more, fell along and northeast of a line from around Center in Knox county to just east and northeast of Council Bluffs in Pottawattamie county in west central Iowa.Heavier amounts in eastern Nebraska included 13.5 inches in Crofton, 10 inches in Walthill and Oakland, 9 inches at Lyons, 8 inches at Wausa and Creighton, 7 inches at Fort Calhoun, Tekamah, West Point, and Blair.Heavier totals in western Iowa included around 13 inches at Castana and near Little Sioux, 11 inches in Mapleton, 9 inches at Harlan and 8 inches in Logan and Kennebec.

An intense low pressure area over the Northern Plains and a strong mid-level jetstream combined to produce a widespread high wind and blowing dirt event across eastern Colorado, western Kansas and parts of SW Nebraska.  Wind gusts of 50 to 65 mph were common from late morning through the afternoon hours causing reduced visibility in blowing dirt.  Many areas suffered minor tree and fence damage and zero visibility at times.  60 mph winds were reported in McCook in Red Willow county.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Five to seven inches of snow fell in Hitchcock county during the early morning hours of the 12th.  Heaviest snow was reported Trenton with 7 inches.  The snow caused inconvenient travel during the morning hours.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The first widespread area of thunderstorms of the season brought rain and hail to the region this Saturday afternoon.  The hail was scattered about south-central Nebraska generally west of U.S. Highway 281 and south of Interstate 80.  The hail ranged from pennies to quarters in size.  Rain amounts were in the one half to one inch range.

Far southeast sections of the county received up to 6 inches of snow.

One of the worst severe weather outbreaks in recent years struck south-central Nebraska the afternoon and evening of May 22. No less than 17 different tornadoes rolled across south-central Nebraska. Dozens of homes were damaged and a few completely destroyed. Over 250 center irrigation pivots were damaged or destroyed in south-central Nebraska alone. Large hail and strong straight-line winds of up to 80 mph also wreaked havoc on the region. Several million dollars in property damage was reported. Hundreds of power poles were snapped resulting in dozens of miles of downed electrical line.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tornado traveled a 7 mile path in southeast Adams county and produced some damage, mainly to center pivot systems.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Widespread severe thunderstorms ripped across most of south-central Nebraska. Tornadoes were reported southwest of Sumner and east of Hebron. High winds and hail were common place. 60 to 80 mph winds rolled across a large part of the area. Buffalo County was without power for a time. A tree fell on one wing of the assisted living center in Ravenna. In Furnas county, large trees were knocked over and several center irrigation pivots were damaged or destroyed. A grain bin was blown away east of Kearney and road signs were blown down throughout Buffalo County. Heavy rain also caused flash flood in Howard and Greeley counties where county roads were damaged or washed out.

Hail up to 1 inch in diameter fell in the Union area.

Heavy rain during the evening of 5/22 produced fairly widespread areas of flash flooding across Madison county for the second night in a row.  Besides flooding county roads, highway 32 east of Madison was closed for a while.  Rainfall of 2 to 3 inches was common throughout the county. Norfolk received 2.44 inches that evening. The rain fell on saturated soils due to 1 to 3 inches of rain that fell across much of the county during the previous night.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe thunderstorms rumbled across south-central Nebraska during the late afternoon and evening hours.  These storms produced hail, strong winds and a few tornadoes.  The storms started out in Gosper county and moved northeast.  There were several reports of funnel clouds in eastern Gosper county.  As the storms continued into Dawson and Buffalo counties, storm chasers continued to report funnels and hail.  The first tornado set down in southeast Sherman county near Rockville and was observed for a few minutes.  The next twister was in southwest Howard county near Farwell.  Another tornado was reported by a sheriff's deputy near the junction of Nebraska highways 92 and 11.  These tornadoes were in open county and caused little in the way of damage.  The town of Elba in Howard county has pummeled by large hail for period of time.  Broken windows and trees stripped of leaves were common throughout the small community.  The most destructive tornado occurred near the village of Cushing.  The tornado set down on a farm west of town and damaged several outbuildings and threw irrigation pipe across a field.  The tornado then cut a path across the south end of town, again damaging several outbuildings and some minor damage to a home.  The tornado dissipated just southeast of town.  Other storms south of these produced strong winds from Grand Island to near Hebron.  Trees and power poles were damaged from the strong winds.

Thunderstorms moving across south-central Nebraska produced little rain, but mainly strong winds. The winds toppled trees in Hall, Howard, Merrick, Polk and Adams counties. Several communities were without power for a time. The wind also stirred dust from freshly plowed fields which resulted in near-zero visibility at times. A 3 vehicle pile-up on the Polk/Butler county line east of Shelby resulted in 9 injuries. The poor visibility due to blowing dust contributed to the cause of the accident.

Water was reported 3 feet deep across Highway 103 near Diller from heavy rain that fell during the evening of the 29th.  Diller received 5.75 inches of rain from the storms that night.
A possible thunderstorm microburst partially tore the roof off of a building, toppled a large tree and blew a parked van into another vehicle all in the same proximity.
Lightning struck 2 men near Zorinsky Lake, seriously injuring 1.
Thunderstorm wind gusts estimated at 60 mph broke large tree limbs across mainly the southwestern sections of town.

The storms started out in Gosper county and moved northeast.  There were several reports of funnel clouds in eastern Gosper county.  As the storms continued into Dawson and Buffalo counties, storm chasers continued to report funnels and hail.  The first tornado set down in southeast Sherman county near Rockville and was observed for a few minutes.  The next twister was in southwest Howard county near Farwell.  Another tornado was reported by a sheriff's deputy near the junction of Nebraska highways 92 and 11.  These tornadoes were in open county and caused little in the way of damage.  The town of Elba in Howard county has pummeled by large hail for period of time.  Broken windows and trees stripped of leaves were common throughout the small community.  The most destructive tornado occurred near the village of Cushing.  The tornado set down on a farm west of town and damaged several outbuildings and threw irrigation pipe across a field.  The tornado then cut a path across the south end of town, again damaging several outbuildings and some minor damage to a home.  The tornado dissipated just southeast of town.  Other storms south of these produced strong winds from Grand Island to near Hebron.  Trees and power poles were damaged from the strong winds.

Widespread severe thunderstorms ripped across most of south-central Nebraska. Tornadoes were reported southwest of Sumner and east of Hebron. High winds and hail were common place. 60 to 80 mph winds rolled across a large part of the area. Buffalo County was without power for a time. A tree fell on one wing of the assisted living center in Ravenna. In Furnas county, large trees were knocked over and several center irrigation pivots were damaged or destroyed. A grain bin was blown away east of Kearney and road signs were blown down throughout Buffalo County. Heavy rain also caused flash flood in Howard and Greeley counties where county roads were damaged or washed out.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The cooperative observer in Genoa reported hail and high winds stripped trees throughout town.

One of the worst severe weather outbreaks in recent years struck south-central Nebraska the afternoon and evening of May 22. No less than 17 different tornadoes rolled across south-central Nebraska. Dozens of homes were damaged and a few completely destroyed. Over 250 center irrigation pivots were damaged or destroyed in south-central Nebraska alone. Large hail and strong straight-line winds of up to 80 mph also wreaked havoc on the region. Several million dollars in property damage was reported. Hundreds of power poles were snapped resulting in dozens of miles of downed electrical line.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: This funnel was reported to be just across the Stanton county line in far southern Wayne county south of Winside. This was from the storm that eventually produced the apparent tornado near Wayne.

A tornado touched down southeast of Wayne causing damage to a house and destroying or severely damaging a barn and 6 outbuildings, along with much of the farm equipment. The debris was strewn for 3 miles downwind.

A tornado was on the ground for around 1.5 miles just west through north of Murdock. The tornado knocked down many trees and power lines in the area, and several outbuildings were destroyed.  This tornado was from the same supercell complex that spawned the long tracked f4 tornado that did extensive damage in the Hallam area.

A tornado uprooted large trees and downed transmission towers from around Nehawka to near Union.  Two houses sustained major damage and "heavy damage" was reported to 30 other houses.  Some of the damage could have also been caused by straight line winds that occurred across parts of the area shortly before the reported time of the tornado.  The strong winds also knocked power out across most of Union.  The track of the tornado was around 4 miles long and was spawned from the same supercell complex that produced the long tracked f4 tornado earlier which caused extensive damage in the Hallam area.

This tornado damaged cabins and homes and downed trees near the Lake Waconda area by Murray.  The tornado was on the ground for about 1 mile, and it was produced from the same supercell complex that spawned 2 earlier tornadoes in Cass county and also the long tracked f4 tornado that caused extensive damage in the Hallam area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Isolated thunderstorms rumbled over south-central Nebraska dropping some hail and producing strong winds. By far, the strongest winds were reported just west of Geneva. A downburst produced wind gusts to 90 mph and damaged two farmsteads. Six grain bins were damaged and at least 1 center pivot system was toppled. Powers lines were snapped just north of Highway 41 on county road 9.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Rainfall of 3 to 6 inches fell across much of the Turkey Creek basin from Friday night the 21st of May through Monday night the 24th.  The heavy rain caused Turkey Creek to surpass its 11 foot flood stage during the morning hours of the 22nd.  The creek crested at 15.85 feet at 800 am Cdt on the 23rd and remained above flood stage until mid afternoon on the 25th.  The flooding caused moderate damage to county roads and flooded agricultural lowlands.
Hail up to 1 inch in diameter stripped leaves from trees on the south side of town.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A trained spotter reported thunderstorm wind gusts estimated at around 70 mph.  The winds were accompanied by golfball size hail.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flash flooding was reported across sections of central and eastern Omaha.  The flooding was caused by heavy rain of 2 to 3 inches which fell across much of the central, eastern and southern sections of the city over a several hour period during the late afternoon hours on the 24th. In fact 2.28 inches was reported at Omaha Eppley which set a record rainfall for the date.  The ground over much of the area was already nearly saturated from a 2 to 3 inch rain that fell two days earlier. Four foot deep flood waters stranded cars in the Saddle Creek and Farnam area, and a health center reported that flood waters flooded a wing of the unit.
Hail up to 1.5 inches in diameter fell around the Dunbar area for several minutes.
Hail up to golfball size fell across the eastern sections of Columbus.
Hail up to 3 inches in diameter was reported in Schuyler.  This was from the same supercell that produced a brief tornado touchdown southwest of town.
A brief touchdown of a tornado was sighted by several trained spotters near the Platte River southwest of Schuyler.  The tornado touched down in open fields and apparently did little or no damage.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Lightning struck a home just west of St. Paul and damaged wiring, electrical appliances, a computer and a television.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Hail up to golfball size fell from 6 miles west northwest of North Bend into the town itself.  The hail was associated with the same supercell that produced the brief tornado touchdown southwest of Schuyler.

Thunderstorm wind gusts estimated at 60 mph were reported in Fremont.  This was from a supercell that produced a tornado touchdown on the eastern sections of the city. The winds knocked down power lines and did tree damage and was accompanied by hail up to 4 inches in diameter.

Hail up to 4 inches in diameter was reported across mainly the northern and eastern sections of the city. The hail was generally nickel to quarter size throughout much of the town, but the larger stones that fell did extensive damage to roofs, siding, windows and trees, again mainly across the northern and eastern sections of town.  The hail was in association with the supercell that produced the brief tornado on the eastern sections of Fremont.

A tornado apparently intermittently touched down from the eastern sections of town where it toppled an air-conditioning unit on top of Walmart onto an SUV parked below, to a farmstead 2 miles east southeast of town where it did extensive damage to farm outbuildings.  Power poles were also snapped off in this path.
Reported by law enforcement.

Heavy rainfall during the evening of the 29th produced flash flooding of many county roads in southern Gage county.  Rainfall amounts included 5.75 inches in Diller, 4.28 inches near Beatrice and 3.15 inches in Barneston.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A small tornado was reported in southeast Thayer county.  This was the final tornado of the day in south-central Nebraska.  Damage was limited to trees, power poles and center pivot irrigation systems.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Lightning struck a home in rural eastern York county. The lightning sparked fire actually smoldered for several hours before engulfing the house shortly after dawn on the 10th. Damage was estimated at $75,000 to the house and $15,000 to the contents.

As a cold front sliced across south-central Nebraska, a supercell thunderstorm developed along the front and moved southeast across Nuckolls and Thayer counties. The supercell became a prolific tornado producing storm with almost a dozen tornadoes having been reported. With rainfall from the thunderstorm limited to a small area, the tornadoes were visible throughout the region by spotters, storm chasers and the public. At one point, three tornadoes were on the ground at one time in southwest Thayer county, one of which severely damaged two farmsteads.

The rains washed out back roads between Pender and Thurston and Highway 77 south of Winnebago was briefly closed by flood waters.
Up to 1 inch hail fell in and around the Gretna area for approximately 10 minutes

Hail up to 1.25 inches in diameter destroyed a swath of corn 1 mile wide and 3 miles long south of Julian.

Rainfall of over 2 inches fell across much of southern Lancaster county in a couple of hours that afternoon causing flash flooding and closure of roads south of Highway 33 west of Martell and west of Hallam.  Hickman reported 2.18 inches of rain from these thunderstorms, and other locations just west or southwest of there probably received more.

Thunderstorm wind gusts estimated up to 80 mph knocked down trees and damaged buildings 2 miles east of Brainard.
Thunderstorm wind gusts estimated at over 60 mph downed large trees that blocked several city streets.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe thunderstorms rumbled across south-central Nebraska during the late afternoon and evening hours.  These storms produced hail, strong winds and a few tornadoes.  The storms started out in Gosper county and moved northeast.  There were several reports of funnel clouds in eastern Gosper county.  As the storms continued into Dawson and Buffalo counties, storm chasers continued to report funnels and hail.  The first tornado set down in southeast Sherman county near Rockville and was observed for a few minutes.  The next twister was in southwest Howard county near Farwell.  Another tornado was reported by a sheriff's deputy near the junction of Nebraska highways 92 and 11.  These tornadoes were in open county and caused little in the way of damage.  The town of Elba in Howard county has pummeled by large hail for period of time.  Broken windows and trees stripped of leaves were common throughout the small community.  The most destructive tornado occurred near the village of Cushing.  The tornado set down on a farm west of town and damaged several outbuildings and threw irrigation pipe across a field.  The tornado then cut a path across the south end of town, again damaging several outbuildings and some minor damage to a home.  The tornado dissipated just southeast of town.  Other storms south of these produced strong winds from Grand Island to near Hebron.  Trees and power poles were damaged from the strong winds.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Emergency management recorded 69 mph winds associated with a thunderstorm. The wind was accompanied by hail a little over 1 inch in diameter and did damage to 30 homes in the area.

A departing line of thunderstorms caused an area of wind damage in the 500 to 700 block of O Street in Lincoln. The winds blew out several windows and did extensive damage to several roofs in the area. Many cars from several car lots in the area sustained damage.  The winds knocked the power out to most of Lincoln for 4 minutes since 3 of the 6 main transmission lines were still down from the May 22nd storm.
This brief tornado touched down at the same time another one was tracking a few miles to the east. This tornado did some tree damage and was caught on film by storm chasers.

This tornado touched down at the same time another one touched down briefly a few miles to the west. This tornado did some tree and farm outbuilding damage over its 2 mile track and was caught on film by storm chasers.
Thunderstorm wind gusts estimated over 60 mph knocked down tree limbs and power poles in Fairbury.
Hail a little over 1 inch in diameter caused significant crop damage from around Oakland to near Craig.
Hail up to 1 inch in diameter broke windshields in town.
There were still drifts of hail visible the next morning in Randolph.
A multiple vortex tornado did some tree damage west of Alvo. It was a mile or two away from another tornado that touched down briefly to the northeast.
A thunderstorm wind gust was measured at 59 mph by the Norfolk Asos.
Hail up to 1 inch in diameter fell for 35 minutes in the Winnebago area.
McCook ASOS
Thunderstorm wind gusts estimated to near 60 mph were reported near Sprague.
A brief tornado touched down and overturned at trailer near Alvo. This brief touchdown was a mile or two away from another weak tornado that was on the ground for a mile or so.
Thunderstorm wind gusts estimated at over 60 mph knocked down a large tree in Brock.
Thunderstorm wind gusts estimated to over 60 mph knocked down a tree in Salem.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: One of the worst severe weather outbreaks in recent years struck south-central Nebraska the afternoon and evening of May 22. No less than 17 different tornadoes rolled across south-central Nebraska. Dozens of homes were damaged and a few completely destroyed. Over 250 center irrigation pivots were damaged or destroyed in south-central Nebraska alone. Large hail and strong straight-line winds of up to 80 mph also wreaked havoc on the region. Several million dollars in property damage was reported. Hundreds of power poles were snapped resulting in dozens of miles of downed electrical line.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tornado set down briefly in far southwest Franklin county near Stamford and damaged a mobile home and upset several irrigation systems.
Thunderstorm wind gusts estimated near 60 mph blew down a tree which landed on a trailer home.
Thunderstorm wind gusts estimated to over 60 mph blew down a few large trees and damaged power lines in Omaha.
Thunderstorm wind gusts of 58 mph were measured near Gretna.

The final tornado reported with this supercell thunderstorm damaged some trees and farm outbuildings south of Elmwood.  In total 5 tornadoes were reported from this supercell that developed in Butler county and tracked into Cass and Otoe counties.  The reported tornadoes were in Lancaster and Cass counties.

Shortly after the tornado touched down 10 miles ESE of Chappell, it hit a farmstead near the intersection of Highway 27 and Interstate 80. Three old, unanchored trailer homes were picked up and moved with two of them completely destroyed. A metal shed was tossed 40 yards and numerous trees were broken or uprooted. As the tornado crossed Interstate 80, it overturned  a vehicle pulling a U-Haul trailer. Two children in the automobile were taken to an area hospital, treated for minor injuries and released. The tornado width increased to 1/8 of a mile as it moved northeast of the interstate. Approximately 8 miles WNW of Big Springs, a center pivot irrigation system was overturned, power poles were broken and a couple of old outbuildings lost their roofs. Baseball size hail also fell on the west side of the storm damaging 2 law enforcement vehicles about 12 miles west of Big Springs.
Strong thunderstorm outflow produced roof damage, took the top of grain bins and blew over chicken houses.  Estimated wind gusts of 80 mph.

Heavy rainfall over the Turkey Creek basin caused the creek to flood for several days. The flooding closed 16 to 18 miles of county roads in Saline county, and caused at least 100 thousand dollars in road damage. Turkey Creek near Wilber crested at 15.6 feet at 1pm on the 14th, flood stage is 11 feet. The creek at Wilber was above flood stage from late evening on the 13th through mid afternoon on the 17th.

Heavy rain that fell across the Little Blue River basin caused the river to flood near Fairbury. Mainly just agricultural lowlands were affected, however.  The river at Fairbury crested at 17 feet at 8pm on the 15th, flood stage is 15 feet.  The river at Fairbury was above flood stage from late morning on the 15th through the early morning hours on the 16th.

Strong thunderstorm winds up to 60 knots knocked down power lines and numerous trees around Broken Bow. Property damage included windows broken out of cars, business signs broken or knocked down, and a metal shed overturned.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Two rounds of severe thunderstorms pounded south-central Nebraska this Saturday.  Crop damage and flooding were common east of U.S. Highway 281 and south of Interstate 80.  The first round began in the morning.  Heavy rain and hail pounded areas from Superior to north of York and Osceola.  Fillmore county was hardest hit with golf ball size hail and one to two inches of rain.The second round by mid-afternoon targeted the sam area primarily east of Hastings.  Fillmore county took the brunt of the storm with 2 to 5 inches of rain and a swath of severe crop damage from west of Milligan to the north of town.  Large hail and heavy rain was also reported at Shickley.  At the time, Fillmore county was busy with activities.  Geneva was having a softball tournament, while Shickley and Milligan were both having town celebrations.In Sutton, 3 to 4 inches of rain fell in a couple of hours.  The School Creek in northeast Clay county spilled over its banks onto U.S. Highway 6.  A bridge was washed out just west of Sutton, and the creek spilled out of its banks in town.  Large hail was also reported in Clay county.

Widespread severe thunderstorms ripped across most of south-central Nebraska. Tornadoes were reported southwest of Sumner and east of Hebron. High winds and hail were common place. 60 to 80 mph winds rolled across a large part of the area. Buffalo County was without power for a time. A tree fell on one wing of the assisted living center in Ravenna. In Furnas county, large trees were knocked over and several center irrigation pivots were damaged or destroyed. A grain bin was blown away east of Kearney and road signs were blown down throughout Buffalo County. Heavy rain also caused flash flood in Howard and Greeley counties where county roads were damaged or washed out.

Several million dollars in property damage was reported. Hundreds of power poles were snapped resulting in dozens of miles of downed electrical line.

As a cold front sliced across south-central Nebraska, a supercell thunderstorm developed along the front and moved southeast across Nuckolls and Thayer counties. The supercell became a prolific tornado producing storm with almost a dozen tornadoes having been reported. With rainfall from the thunderstorm limited to a small area, the tornadoes were visible throughout the region by spotters, storm chasers and the public. At one point, three tornadoes were on the ground at one time in southwest Thayer county, one of which severely damaged two farmsteads.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A brief tornado was reported by the local power company.  A couple of power poles were snapped.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Rainfall of over 2 inches in a several hour period caused flash flooding across parts of Otoe county, especially from west of Syracuse to west of Nebraska City, where a couple of roads were flooded.  Otoe received almost 2.5 inches of rain from the storms for one of the heavier amounts reported.

Heavy rain caused flash flooding and closures of Highways 91 and 14 near Albion, Highway 32 around Petersburg and Highway 52 east of Primrose.  Flooding and debris was also reported for a while for across Highway 56.  The flooding caused some cars and trucks to go into ditches. Many county roads were also flooded in the northern part of the county.  Just north of Primrose 3.5 inches of rain fell that night, with 2.57 inches reported in Lorretto.  Standing water remained over many county roads through much of the 17th.
A brief tornado touchdown was observed by fire and rescue units west of Cedar Bluffs.  The tornado did no apparent damage.

Severe thunderstorms rumbled across south-central Nebraska during the late afternoon and evening hours.  These storms produced hail, strong winds and a few tornadoes.  The storms started out in Gosper county and moved northeast.  There were several reports of funnel clouds in eastern Gosper county.  As the storms continued into Dawson and Buffalo counties, storm chasers continued to report funnels and hail.  The first tornado set down in southeast Sherman county near Rockville and was observed for a few minutes.  The next twister was in southwest Howard county near Farwell.  Another tornado was reported by a sheriff's deputy near the junction of Nebraska highways 92 and 11.  These tornadoes were in open county and caused little in the way of damage.  The town of Elba in Howard county has pummeled by large hail for period of time.  Broken windows and trees stripped of leaves were common throughout the small community.  The most destructive tornado occurred near the village of Cushing.  The tornado set down on a farm west of town and damaged several outbuildings and threw irrigation pipe across a field.  The tornado then cut a path across the south end of town, again damaging several outbuildings and some minor damage to a home.  The tornado dissipated just southeast of town.  Other storms south of these produced strong winds from Grand Island to near Hebron.  Trees and power poles were damaged from the strong winds.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: 1 to 1 1/2 inch hail.

Strong outflow boundary from thunderstorms in NE Colorado and SW Nebraska swept rapidly southeast across parts of southwest Nebraska. Winds of 55 to 70 mph accompanied the outflow, including visibility reduced to 1/4 mile in blowing dirt.  Power poles were knocked down on the east side of Trenton according to newspaper reports, with large tree limbs down in Palisade, Trenton, Stratton and Culbertson.

Strong outflow boundary from thunderstorms in NE Colorado and SW Nebraska swept rapidly southeast across parts of southwest Nebraska. Winds of 55 to 70 mph accompanied the outflow, including visibility reduced to 1/4 mile in blowing dirt.  Power poles were knocked down on the east side of Trenton according to newspaper reports, with large tree limbs down in Palisade, Trenton, Stratton and Culbertson.
Severe thunderstorms dropped hail, generally the size of nickels or smaller across parts of south-central Nebraska.  One storm did drop hail to the size of golf balls around the town of Grand Island.

Widespread severe thunderstorms ripped across most of south-central Nebraska. Tornadoes were reported southwest of Sumner and east of Hebron. High winds and hail were common place. 60 to 80 mph winds rolled across a large part of the area. Buffalo County was without power for a time. A tree fell on one wing of the assisted living center in Ravenna. In Furnas county, large trees were knocked over and several center irrigation pivots were damaged or destroyed. A grain bin was blown away east of Kearney and road signs were blown down throughout Buffalo County. Heavy rain also caused flash flood in Howard and Greeley counties where county roads were damaged or washed out.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The cooperative observer in Ashton reported penny size hail fell for about ten minutes.

Severe thunderstorms rumbled across south-central Nebraska during the late afternoon and evening hours.  These storms produced hail, strong winds and a few tornadoes.  The storms started out in Gosper county and moved northeast.  There were several reports of funnel clouds in eastern Gosper county.  As the storms continued into Dawson and Buffalo counties, storm chasers continued to report funnels and hail.  The first tornado set down in southeast Sherman county near Rockville and was observed for a few minutes.  The next twister was in southwest Howard county near Farwell.  Another tornado was reported by a sheriff's deputy near the junction of Nebraska highways 92 and 11.  These tornadoes were in open county and caused little in the way of damage.  The town of Elba in Howard county has pummeled by large hail for period of time.  Broken windows and trees stripped of leaves were common throughout the small community.  The most destructive tornado occurred near the village of Cushing.  The tornado set down on a farm west of town and damaged several outbuildings and threw irrigation pipe across a field.  The tornado then cut a path across the south end of town, again damaging several outbuildings and some minor damage to a home.  The tornado dissipated just southeast of town.  Other storms south of these produced strong winds from Grand Island to near Hebron.  Trees and power poles were damaged from the strong winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Tornado crossed Highway 11 west of St. Paul and was observed by law enforcement.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A brief tornado was reported southwest of Sumner in Dawson county.  The tornado occurred in open country.
The brief tornado touched down in open rangeland.
The tornado touched down and remained over open rangeland.
Hail broke 32 roof panels and 97 windows in greenhouses.
Hail accumulated to 2 to 3 inches deep on Highway 83.
Numerous six inch diameter tree limbs were down just north of Palisade.
Numerous trees and power poles were broken off in Oconto.

Hail completely shredded wheat fields and newly planted corn northwest of Oconto.  Roofs and siding on nearly every home and business were severely damaged.  Vehicles sustained severe body damage along with broken windows.
Large tree limbs were broken off along with a few trees uprooted along Interstate 80.
The tornado touched down in an open field.

Prolonged flooding continued over northern Platte county, especially from around Lindsay to near Platte Center, after the flash flooding subsided early Saturday morning.  Most of the flooding was along Shell Creek and Tracey Creek where several county roads remained closed into Monday.  Water also flowed across parts of Highway 81 near Platte Center and Highway 91 west of Lindsay.  Both highways were either closed or had travel restricted to one lane for a while.  The Platte Center fire station reported around 8 inches of rain over the weekend, and county road employees logged around 100 hours of overtime.  Damage was at least 8000 to 10000 dollars to county roads and bridges.
An Amatuer radio operator reported a funnel cloud just north of Nebraska City.

Heavy rain fell across much of Knox county this evening which produced flash flooding over many gravel roads across the county.  The water also flooded across Highway 13 south of Creighton and Highway 59 west of town.  A lot of standing water remained on the Creighton golf course well into Sunday the 30th.
Heavy rain during the evening of the 29th flooded many county roads across the county.
Thunderstorm wind gusts of 60 mph broke large tree limbs north of Auburn.
Heavy rain during the evening of the 29th produced flash flooding across a few county roads.
Heavy rain this evening produced flash flooding, especially east and south of Fairbury where some roads were flooded.  Fairbury reported 3.75 inches of rain from the storms.
Thunderstorm wind gusts estimated at 65 mph downed large tree limbs in town.
Rainfall of 2 to 4 inches fell across much of Nemaha county this evening producing areas of flash flooding at least several inches deep across numerous county roads.
Thunderstorm wind gusts estimated at 60 mph downed some medium size tree limbs in town.
A thunderstorm wind gust estimated at over 60 mph blew down a tree across a road near Rising City.
A thunderstorm wind gust of 61 mph was recorded at the NWS office in Valley.
A thunderstorm wind gust of 67 mph was measured by ASOS at Omaha Eppley Airfield.
Thunderstorm winds estimated at least 60 mph downed some large tree limbs in town.
Thunderstorm wind gusts were estimated at 60 mph near Springfield by Law Enforcement.
