EVENT_NARRATIVE: A swath of 2 to 4 inches of rain fell across Boone county from the evening of the 21st into the early morning hours on the 22nd.  This produced flash flooding across several county roads, and washed sections out of a few.  Six to 8 inches of flood waters also crossed Highway 14 in Albion.  The damage to county roads alone was estimated at around 100 thousand dollars.

Two to three inches of rain that fell across a large part of Dodge county during the late afternoon and evening of 5/22 caused flash flooding, especially across the northern sections of the county.  Dodge reported 2.27 inches of rain in less than 2 hours that evening which caused flash flooding in town including spur 27A leading into town.  Flash flooding was also reported between Dodge and Snyder where a woman had to be rescued when her car became stranded by flood waters.  Flooding was also observed near the junction of Highways 91 and 275.   The rain fell on soils that were saturated from 1 to 3 inches of rain that fell during the previous night.
Hail up to 1 inch in diameter fell in the Weston area.

Severe thunderstorms tracked across Omaha producing hail up to 1.25 inches in diameter and wind gusts estimated as high as 60 mph.  Many of the larger hail reports were across sections of northwest Omaha, from around 132nd and Fort Streets toward Lake Cunningham, and also in the Millard area. However, scattered hail reports of at least 3/4 inch in diameter were received from much of the city.  The strongest wind gusts reported were near 162nd and Q Streets where 2 trees were uprooted.

Thunderstorms which tracked across Platte county much of the night on the 21st into the morning hours on the 22nd produced areas of flash flooding.  The rainfall was heaviest across the northern sections of the county where most of the flooding was observed.  Humphrey recorded 3.35 inches of rain from these storms while Lindsay reported around 4 inches.  The rains flooded county roads around both towns.  Highway 81 south of Humphrey was partially flooded and other flooding was reported along Shell Creek from Lindsay to near Platte Center.  A few gravel roads were washed out around Platte Center and Humphrey.

Rainfall of 2 to 4 inches, which fell over a large part of Lancaster county Saturday night 5/22/04, besides producing areas of flash flooding, caused Salt Creek to come out of its banks flooding roads, basements and agricultural lands around the Roca area the next day.  Salt Creek at Roca crested at 20 feet at 7 am Cdt on the 23rd, flood stage is 19 feet.  The creek was above flood stage from around 115 am Cdt through 415 pm on May 23rd.
Heavy rain May 29th produced flooding along Bow Creek, especially near Wynot where several county roads were flooded.  Agricultural lowlands were also flooded.

Thunderstorm wind gusts estimated at 70 to 80 mph overturned a truck trailer and flattened several grain bins near Princeton.  The winds were probably associated with the supercell that was producing a tornado around this same time just to the south of town.
A tornado overturned several irrigation pivots and grain bins and also did some minor house damage from southwest through just east of Daykin.

A small tornado touched down at the same time the much larger and longer lived Hallam tornado was occurring several miles to the west.  This tornado was on the ground for around 2 miles and caused some minor damage to farm outbuildings.
Thunderstorm wind gusts estimated at 60 mph uprooted 2 trees near 162nd and Q Streets.  Hail reports of 3/4 to 1.25 inches in diameter were recieved across Omaha from this storm.
Flash flooding from 2 to 3 inches of rain flooded across Highway 77 near Princeton and roads around the Roca area.

Rainfall of 2 to 3 inches fell across much of Stanton county during the evening of 5/22. The rains fell on saturated soil from 1 to 2 inches of rain that fell the previous night.  This produced widespread flash flooding throughout the county.  Many county roads were flooded and Highway 32 was closed for a while.  Emergency managment reported that at least 1500 acres of land were under water across the southern part of the county.

Heavy rainfall from thunderstorms during the evening of the 21st into the early morning hours on the 22nd produced flash flooding across several county roads in Thurston county.  Pender picked up around 3.5 inches of rain from the storms.

Rainfall of at least 2 to 3 inches fell over a large part of Cuming county from the evening of the 21st into the early morning hours on the 22nd.  The rain produced areas of flash flooding across at least several roads and highways in the county.  Flood waters crossed highway 51 north of Beemer and highway 9 in the north part of the county near the Thurston county line.  Both highways were briefly closed.  County roads 285 and 4 1/2 were also reported flooded.
Thunderstorm wind gusts of 60 to 70 mph were estimated by an Amateur radio operator just north of Dunbar.  The winds were accompanied by hail around nickel size.
Mostly pea-size but a few reached dime size.
Heavy rain produced areas of flash flooding in Boone county, especially from west of Albion toward the Loretto area where a road was washed out and several other roads were flooded.
A brief tornado touchdown was reported by emergency management in southwest Pawnee county.
A lightning strike caused fire in an attic and top floor of a house in Lincoln.
Thunderstorm wind gusts estimated at over 60 mph overturned 9 center irrigation pivots and destroyed several grain bins north of Petersburg.

Thunderstorm wind gusts estimated at over 80 mph tore up large tree limbs west of Union.  The winds were possibly the rear flank downdraft from a developing tornado that tracked from Nehawka to near Union.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: This long tracked tornado is often referred to as the Hallam tornado.  It initially touched down 3 miles west of Daykin in northern Jefferson county.  The tornado was rated an f0 or f1 in Jefferson county damaging farm outbuildings, grain bins and trees. From there the tornado crossed into Saline county southwest of Western and remained an f0 or f1 until it struck the southern portion of Wilber where it strengthened to f2. Roofs were blown off of homes just southeast of Wilber.The tornado traveled from Wilber into Gage county, crossing the county line west of Clatonia where it grew to its most intense stage, f4.  The tornado remained nearly at this strength as it crossed into Lancaster county near Hallam with a damage path of around 2 1/2 miles.  Many well-built homes were demolished from Clatonia to Hallam, along with grain bins, farm sheds, and outbuildings.  Many trees were destroyed or uprooted.  Although Hallam itself escaped the strongest winds from the storm, which occurred just south of town, 95 percent of the buildings in town were either destroyed or severely damaged.  The lone fatality from the tornado occurred in Hallam.  The storm also toppled several hopper cars from a freight train on the west edge of town.  In total 55 railroad cars were derailed.From Hallam the tornado traveled east for several miles prior to turning northeast again just north of Cortland. The storm then tracked 2 miles north of Firth, severely damaging the Firth-Norris high school and a nearby middle school.  School busses were tossed in this area.  Several homes northeast of the schools were flattened as the tornado regained its f4 strength.The damage path continued northeast to Holland and then to 2 miles north of Panama where the tornado weakened to around an f2 and the damage path began to narrow.  The track then curved more toward the north, passing just south of Bennet where a few homes sustained f3 damage.  After passing south of Bennet, the storm moved back to the northeast and began to weaken to f0 or f1 strength as it crossed into Otoe county southwest of Palmyra.  The tornado finally dissipated 1 miles west southwest of Palmyra.In total the tornado was on the ground for around 54 miles with a maximum intensity of f4.  Besides the fatality, 38 people sustained injuries, 158 homes were leveled and 57 others were seriously damaged. The dollar amount of damage was estimated at 160 million, with 60 million of that agricultural including 100 cattle and 50 hogs lost.  Some 150,000 acres of crop land sustained significant damage.  The 5 counties were declared national disaster areas by Fema.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: See description below

The lone fatality from the tornado occurred in Hallam.  The storm also toppled several hopper cars from a freight train on the west edge of town.  In total 55 railroad cars were derailed.From Hallam the tornado traveled east for several miles prior to turning northeast again just north of Cortland. The storm then tracked 2 miles north of Firth, severely damaging the Firth-Norris high school and a nearby middle school.  School busses were tossed in this area.  Several homes northeast of the schools were flattened as the tornado regained its f4 strength.The damage path continued northeast to Holland and then to 2 miles north of Panama where the tornado weakened to around an f2 and the damage path began to narrow.  The track then curved more toward the north, passing just south of Bennet where a few homes sustained f3 damage.  After passing south of Bennet, the storm moved back to the northeast and began to weaken to f0 or f1 strength as it crossed into Otoe county southwest of Palmyra.  The tornado finally dissipated 1 miles west southwest of Palmyra.In total the tornado was on the ground for around 54 miles with a maximum intensity of f4.  Besides the fatality, 38 people sustained injuries, 158 homes were leveled and 57 others were seriously damaged. The dollar amount of damage was estimated at 160 million, with 60 million of that agricultural including 100 cattle and 50 hogs lost.  Some 150,000 acres of crop land sustained significant damage.  The 5 counties were declared national disaster areas by Fema.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: See description below.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: This long tracked tornado is often referred to as the Hallam tornado.  It initially touched down 3 miles west of Daykin in northern Jefferson county.  The tornado was rated an f0 or f1 in Jefferson county damaging farm outbuildings, grain bins and trees. From there the tornado crossed into Saline county southwest of Western and remained an f0 or f1 until it struck the southern portion of Wilber where it strengthened to f2. Roofs were blown off of homes just southeast of Wilber.The tornado traveled from Wilber into Gage county, crossing the county line west of Clatonia where it grew to its most intense stage, f4.  The tornado remained nearly at this strength as it crossed into Lancaster county near Hallam with a damage path of around 2 1/2 miles. EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain caused flash flooding across southern Madison county...especially around the Newman Grove area where flood waters closed highway 45 near town.  Rainfall of at least 3 inches fell across southern Madison county during the overnight hours from the 21st into the 22nd, with Newman Grove reporting 4.5 inches.
Large trees were uprooted and power lines were knocked down.  An open pole barn used for storage at a lumber yard on the east side of North Platte was blown over.
Emergency management estimated thunderstorm wind gusts of 60 mph north of Fairbury.
The tornado touched down briefly over open rangeland.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A rapidly deepening low pressure resulted in widespread winds of 40 to 60 mph across all of south central Nebraska. Wind gusts of 60 mph were reported at Kearney and Lexington, 58 mph at Holdrege, 56 mph at Grand Island and 55 mph at Hastings. Damage was sporadic over the region. A sign was ripped from a furniture store in Holdrege. In Grand Island, a light pole broke at an auto dealership and damaged four vehicles as it fell to the ground. Damage to the vehicles along was estimated at $14,000. There were numerous reports of tree damage and wind power line damage elsewhere in the area. The wind also kicked up dirt from freshly plowed fields. As a result, visibilities were down to 2 to 3 miles at times.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain produced flash flooding across many of the county's rural roads, especially around the Carroll, Wayne and Winside areas.  Some of the roads were washed out.  Rainfall of nearly 2 inches in less than 2 hours was reported in Wayne. Rainfall amounts were probably higher in the eastern parts of the county as Pender, just across the county line in Thurston county, had around 3.5 inches.
The tornado touched down briefly in open rangeland.
Thunderstorm winds in excess of 60 mph caused damage near Swanton.  The winds were accompanied by hail up to baseball size.

Thunderstorm winds caused damage to parts of Wilber including knocking the power out and forcing the evacuation of the EOC. The winds were probably around 70 mph and were possibly due to the RFD of a large tornado that was passing just to the south of town around that time.

Thunderstorm wind gusts of at least 60 mph were reported near the junction of Highways 77 and 41 in northern Gage county.  The winds were probably associated with a supercell thunderstorm that produced a large and long-tracked tornado that was passing just north of that location around that time.

Rainfall of 2 to 3 inches which fell over parts of Saline county during the evening of 5/22 produced areas of flash flooding across Saline county.  An area near Dorchester reported local flooding of roads near town.
The tornado moved across open pastures.
The tornado moved across open fields and pastures.
Runoff from heavy rain accumulated quickly and washed out fences as it moved through low lying areas.  Water rose up to the headlights of cars on Main Street.

The tornado took down power poles and uprooted numerous trees south of state highway 95 and then hit a farmstead as it crossed the highway about 3 miles south of Amelia ripping off the roof of a metal outbuilding and depositing it on the road.  As it moved east-northeast, it overturned a center pivot irrigation system and uprooted more trees.
The tornado made a brief touchdown over open rangeland.
The tornado touched down briefly in an open pasture.
A cooperative observer reported that thunderstorm winds estimated at around 60 mph knocked down a few large tree limbs in town.
Thunderstorm wind gusts estimated at over 60 mph downed large tree limbs near the Alexandria Lakes State Recreation Area.
Hail 1 inch in diameter was reported by the Emergency Manager near the Rock Creek State Historical Park.

Rainfall of 2 to 3 inches fell across much of Pierce county during the evening of 5/22. The rains fell on saturated soils due to 1 to 2 inches of rain that fell during the previous night.  The rain produced flash flooding in the town of Pierce which closed Highway 13 in town for a while.
Thunderstorm wind gusts estimated at 70 mph by emergency management were accompanied by dime size hail.
Thunderstorm wind gusts estimated at around 60 mph downed large tree limbs near Odell.
Hail up to baseball size knocked car windshields out, broke windows on a few homes and did siding damage.
Hail up to dime size covered the ground north of Crofton.
Large tree limbs were broken by thunderstorm winds estimated at 60 mph.
Hail up to 3 inches in diameter fell from just southwest of Tobias into town.
One inch in diameter hail was reported from 3 miles west through 4 miles north of Fairbury.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: For the second time in less than a week heavy rain over southeast Nebraska and southwest Iowa caused the Missouri River to flood from around Brownville through Rulo.  The river crested at Brownville at 32.1 feet at 215 pm Cdt on the 30th, flood stage is 32 feet.  The river at Rulo crested at 19.4 feet at 200 pm Cdt May 30th, flood stage is 17 feet.  The flooding affected mainly agricultural lowlands.

Thunderstorm wind gusts estimated at over 60 mph destroyed a shed and did some tree damage in Hickman.

A trained weather spotter reported a brief tornado touchdown south of Clarkson. The tornado did no apparent damage.
Thunderstorm wind gusts of around 60 mph downed a large walnut tree in town which damaged a house as it fell.

Heavy rains over a weeks time caused Beaver Creek near St. Edward to flood from late evening on May 29th though early evening on the 30th.  The flooding closed several county roads, but otherwise just flooded agricultural lowlands.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Two rounds of severe thunderstorms pounded south-central Nebraska this Saturday.  Crop damage and flooding were common east of U.S. Highway 281 and south of Interstate 80.  The first round began in the morning.  Heavy rain and hail pounded areas from Superior to north of York and Osceola.  Fillmore county was hardest hit with golf ball size hail and one to two inches of rain.The second round by mid-afternoon targeted the sam area primarily east of Hastings.  Fillmore county took the brunt of the storm with 2 to 5 inches of rain and a swath of severe crop damage from west of Milligan to the north of town.  Large hail and heavy rain was also reported at Shickley.  At the time, Fillmore county was busy with activities.  Geneva was having a softball tournament, while Shickley and Milligan were both having town celebrations.In Sutton, 3 to 4 inches of rain fell in a couple of hours.  The School Creek in northeast Clay county spilled over its banks onto U.S. Highway 6.  A bridge was washed out just west of Sutton, and the creek spilled out of its banks in town.  Large hail was also reported in Clay county.

A night of severe weather pounded sections of south-central Nebraska with two tornadoes and several reports of hail and high winds.  The first tornado occurred in Webster county.  The path stretched from south of Inavale to northeast of Red Cloud near Cowles.  Along its path, a couple of pivots were overturned, several out buildings ripped apart and windows blown out of a home northwest of Red Cloud.  The tornado crossed U.S. Highway 136 west of Red Cloud and U.S. Highway 281 north of town.  Property damage along the tornado's 12 mile path was estimated at one-half million dollars.Elsewhere in south-central Nebraska, a brief tornado tore a 40 foot covering from a house north of Boelus.  The homeowners said the twister left a visible trail of damage through a stand of cottonwood trees to the northeast of their property.  High winds caused minor damage in Kearney.  Large hail was reported in Furnas county, but that was offset by much needed rain in the area.

Severe thunderstorms cut a swath of crop damage which resulted in millions of losses.  The main area of damage was in northeast Gosper, southeast Dawson and northwest Phelps counties.  In Dawson county, 10,000 acres were wiped out with a total loss of over four million dollars. Losses in Phelps and Gosper counties were from 2 to 2.5 million dollars respectively.  In Lexington, the hail covered the ground at one point.  Flash flood waters from heavy rain were up to 2 feet deep for a time.  Near Ravenna, crop damage from wind and hail was reported.  This is just one of several severe thunderstorms which inflected severe crop damage in the Ravenna area this summer.

A large severe thunderstorm took aim on a portion of south-central Nebraska and produced a tornado and extensive property and crop damage due to hail and high winds.  The thunderstorm initially ripped across Greeley county.  Damage was noted throughout almost the entire county.  Of note, hangers at the Greeley Airport sustained damage, a large grain bin at the County Partners Co-op collapsed, pivots were overturned and windows broken out and roofs damaged.  A communications tower west of Wolbach was toppled.  The tower had withstood 42 years of Nebraska weather before collapsing during this storm.  Flooding was reported in southeast Greeley county near Wolbach.In Howard county, a brief tornado west of St. Paul destroyed a couple of grain bins.  North of town, a tree fell into the living room of one house.  Tree and property damage was widespread throughout St. Paul.  To the west, large hail was reported in Sherman and Buffalo counties.  Crop damage was heavy in many locations.  A 60 mph wind was recorded at the Central Nebraska Regional Airport near Grand Island before the thunderstorm dissipated.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A supercell storm that produced a brief tornado 7 minutes earlier dropped large hail as it moved over the area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe thunderstorms produced widespread damage to trees and structures in south central Nebraska. The worst damage occurred near Central City. 90 mph winds destroyed five grain bins and ripped about 20 rafters from a farm house near town. The occupants were uninjured. The next morning, nearly 70 volunteers assisted in the clean-up of over 30 tons of mangled steel. Most of the damage in the area was from trees falling on power lines, cars and houses. A tree fell on a house and into the bedroom of a little girl in Polk County. The girl was uninjured. In Furnas County, heavy rain and the eventual runoff caused flooding on the Turkey Creek east of Edison during the predawn hours.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong thunderstorm winds up to 60 knots downed power lines and numerous trees.
Strong thunderstorm winds up to 50 knots brought tree limbs down and several outbuildings were blown over.

Numerous large trees were snapped off with a few of them falling onto parked vehicles or into buildings.  An airplane hanger was destroyed, a portion of a roof was blown off a lumberyard storage shed, and 31 power poles were also broken off by the intense thunderstorm winds.

Strong winds accompanied the large hail to break windows in homes and vehicles, damage roofs and bodies of vehicles, and destroy corn crops.

Severe thunderstorms produced widespread damage to trees and structures in south central Nebraska. The worst damage occurred near Central City. 90 mph winds destroyed five grain bins and ripped about 20 rafters from a farm house near town. The occupants were uninjured. The next morning, nearly 70 volunteers assisted in the clean-up of over 30 tons of mangled steel. Most of the damage in the area was from trees falling on power lines, cars and houses. A tree fell on a house and into the bedroom of a little girl in Polk County. The girl was uninjured. In Furnas County, heavy rain and the eventual runoff caused flooding on the Turkey Creek east of Edison during the predawn hours.

Severe thunderstorms cut a swath of crop damage which resulted in millions of losses.  The main area of damage was in northeast Gosper, southeast Dawson and northwest Phelps counties.  In Dawson county, 10,000 acres were wiped out with a total loss of over four million dollars. Losses in Phelps and Gosper counties were from 2 to 2.5 million dollars respectively.  In Lexington, the hail covered the ground at one point.  Flash flood waters from heavy rain were up to 2 feet deep for a time.  Near Ravenna, crop damage from wind and hail was reported.  This is just one of several severe thunderstorms which inflected severe crop damage in the Ravenna area this summer.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A night of severe weather pounded sections of south-central Nebraska with two tornadoes and several reports of hail and high winds.  The first tornado occurred in Webster county.  The path stretched from south of Inavale to northeast of Red Cloud near Cowles.  Along its path, a couple of pivots were overturned, several out buildings ripped apart and windows blown out of a home northwest of Red Cloud.  The tornado crossed U.S. Highway 136 west of Red Cloud and U.S. Highway 281 north of town.  Property damage along the tornado's 12 mile path was estimated at one-half million dollars.Elsewhere in south-central Nebraska, a brief tornado tore a 40 foot covering from a house north of Boelus.  The homeowners said the twister left a visible trail of damage through a stand of cottonwood trees to the northeast of their property.  High winds caused minor damage in Kearney.  Large hail was reported in Furnas county, but that was offset by much needed rain in the area.

Thunderstorm wind gusts over 60 mph plowed across Dawson county.  In Lexington, the winds were strong enough to bend the sign of the Comfort Inn motel.  The sign was approximately 50 feet tall.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: 50 to 60 mph winds were also reported at the same time.
Lightning struck a house in northwest Omaha causing an attic fire which destroyed a room in the attic.

Heavy rainfall in the Omaha metropolitan area caused areas of flash flooding, especially near the Saddle Creek and Center Streets area, near the intersections of 96th and Q Streets, 17th and Ames Avenue and 108th and Q streets.  It was near that last location where a 29-year-old male apparently drowned after his car became stalled in flood waters and he was swept away in a nearby drainage ditch as he was walking for help.  His body was found more than 20 miles downstream in the Missouri River after it was surmised that his body traveled from the ditch into the Big Papillion Creek and then eventually into the Missouri River.  Other flooding occurred when a pool of water 6 feet high developed near the Saddle Creek location carrying off a few vehicles and flooding at least one apartment complex.  Several businesses were flooded and sewers backed up into properties.  Eppley Airfield reported 2.66 inches of rain from the storm in just a several hour period, while some locations in midtown Omaha received almost 3.5 inches of rain.

Thunderstorm wind gusts estimated at least 60 mph downed a large tree in Pawnee City and also downed many tree limbs.  The winds also blew a window out of a vacant building, and a few cars were damaged by fallen tree limbs.  Trees and power poles were reported broken on county roads 2 miles west and 2.5 miles north of town.
Heavy rain from an intense thunderstorm caused six inches of water to flow over portions of Highway 2 in Thedford.
Trees and power poles were snapped off and a metal shed was tossed by the strong outflow winds.
Hail ranging in size from golf ball to softball was associated with a supercell thunderstorm that produced a tornado approximately 2 to 3 miles to the southeast.
The Valley County Emergency Manager observed a brief tornado in southern Valley county.  The tornado did no damage.
A funnel cloud was observed northeast of Waco.

Thunderstorm wind gusts over 60 mph plowed across Dawson county.  In Lexington, the winds were strong enough to bend the sign of the Comfort Inn motel.  The sign was approximately 50 feet tall.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A large tree was knocked down by the strong wind gusts.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: 70 mph winds were reported with the hail.  Crop damage was significant in the area.

The tornado touched down in a cornfield and overturned a center pivot irrigation system as it moved west southwestwards.  Nearby, large tree limbs were broken off and a roof was taken off an open detached garage at a farmstead.  Then the tornado encountered a second farmstead where it lifted an unanchored manufactured home and smashed it on top of a camper trailer and a detached garage, destroying them.  It also lifted a semi-trailer and dropped it onto a John Deere tractor and a pickup sitting next to it.  Debris from the house and garage was carried into the cornfield to the west.  The tornado continued moving west southwestwards breaking tree limbs before lifting.  A husband, wife, and their daughter were inside the home when it was destroyed.  The woman was hospitalized with a broken neck and the girl required numerous stitches from lacerations on her leg.  The man only received minor cuts and bruises.

The tornado touched down in open rangeland and then moved south breaking off and uprooting numerous large trees as it encountered a farmstead.  One large tree fell on the roof of a home.  As the tornado moved south across the road, it hit another farmstead.  Again, it broke off and uprooted numerous large trees and broke many power poles.  The barn and steel fencing was lifted and tossed 50 yards northwestwards into a tree belt behind the house.  The roof of the home was taken off.  A detached garage was demolished.  Before lifting, the tornado moved through a corral south of the home where it severely injured or killed several horses.  After the storm, two colts and a mare were found up in a tree.
The tornado touched down in an open pasture and then traveled southwest breaking off trees and tearing out fences.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm wind gusts over 60 mph plowed across Dawson county.  In Lexington, the winds were strong enough to bend the sign of the Comfort Inn motel.  The sign was approximately 50 feet tall.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Lightning struck Christmas lights which were still up on a Bellevue home. The lightning then traveled along a gutter and ignited a gas meter on the side of the house which was leaking natural gas. The resulting fire did minimal exterior damage.
A woman was slightly injured when lightning struck the ground near where she was standing.
Thunderstorm wind gusts estimated at 60 mph uprooted trees or broke large branches in Irvington which downed power lines and knocked the power out to one or two dozen homes in the area.
Heavy rain produced flash flooding across several county roads southwest of St. Edward.

Thunderstorm wind gusts estimated at 70 mph downed a few large trees and tree branches in town causing some vehicle and house damage.  A fallen tree hit a power substation knocking the power out to the entire town for around 2 hours.
Thunderstorm wind gusts estimated at 60 mph broke large tree branches in town.

The main area of damage was in northeast Gosper, southeast Dawson and northwest Phelps counties.  In Dawson county, 10,000 acres were wiped out with a total loss of over four million dollars. Losses in Phelps and Gosper counties were from 2 to 2.5 million dollars respectively.  In Lexington, the hail covered the ground at one point.  Flash flood waters from heavy rain were up to 2 feet deep for a time.  Near Ravenna, crop damage from wind and hail was reported.  This is just one of several severe thunderstorms which inflected severe crop damage in the Ravenna area this summer.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Hail accompanied the strong thunderstorm winds.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Rainfall of 2.5 to 3.5 inches fell across a large part of the Papillion Creek basin during the early morning hours on the 22nd.  The heavy rains caused areas of flash flooding across parts of Omaha, which apparently resulted in the death of one man who was washed away in a ditch that eventually fed the Big Papillion Creek and then the Missouri River where his body was found. After the flash flooding subsided, flooding was reported along the Big Papillion Creek, which at Fort Crook reached 28.3 feet at 930 am Cdt, flood stage is 27 feet.  The creek at Fort Crook was above flood stage for around 2 1/2 hours that morning, eventually falling below flood stage at 1100 am Cdt.  Although not officially counted as either a flash flood or flood drowning victim in this publication, another person drowned during this event when his kayak flipped over in high water.  He was kayaking in one of the branches of the Papillion creek system after the waters begin subsiding .

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Two rounds of severe thunderstorms pounded south-central Nebraska this Saturday.  Crop damage and flooding were common east of U.S. Highway 281 and south of Interstate 80.  The first round began in the morning.  Heavy rain and hail pounded areas from Superior to north of York and Osceola.  Fillmore county was hardest hit with golf ball size hail and one to two inches of rain.The second round by mid-afternoon targeted the sam area primarily east of Hastings.  Fillmore county took the brunt of the storm with 2 to 5 inches of rain and a swath of severe crop damage from west of Milligan to the north of town.  Large hail and heavy rain was also reported at Shickley.  At the time, Fillmore county was busy with activities.  Geneva was having a softball tournament, while Shickley and Milligan were both having town celebrations.In Sutton, 3 to 4 inches of rain fell in a couple of hours.  The School Creek in northeast Clay county spilled over its banks onto U.S. Highway 6.  A bridge was washed out just west of Sutton, and the creek spilled out of its banks in town.  Large hail was also reported in Clay county.

A night of severe weather pounded sections of south-central Nebraska with two tornadoes and several reports of hail and high winds.  The first tornado occurred in Webster county.  The path stretched from south of Inavale to northeast of Red Cloud near Cowles.  Along its path, a couple of pivots were overturned, several out buildings ripped apart and windows blown out of a home northwest of Red Cloud.  The tornado crossed U.S. Highway 136 west of Red Cloud and U.S. Highway 281 north of town.  Property damage along the tornado's 12 mile path was estimated at one-half million dollars.Elsewhere in south-central Nebraska, a brief tornado tore a 40 foot covering from a house north of Boelus.  The homeowners said the twister left a visible trail of damage through a stand of cottonwood trees to the northeast of their property.  High winds caused minor damage in Kearney.  Large hail was reported in Furnas county, but that was offset by much needed rain in the area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe thunderstorms produced widespread damage to trees and structures in south central Nebraska. The worst damage occurred near Central City. 90 mph winds destroyed five grain bins and ripped about 20 rafters from a farm house near town. The occupants were uninjured. The next morning, nearly 70 volunteers assisted in the clean-up of over 30 tons of mangled steel. Most of the damage in the area was from trees falling on power lines, cars and houses. A tree fell on a house and into the bedroom of a little girl in Polk County. The girl was uninjured. In Furnas County, heavy rain and the eventual runoff caused flooding on the Turkey Creek east of Edison during the predawn hours.

Storm chasers reported a brief tornado touchdown in open rangeland.

Severe thunderstorms rumbled across south-central Nebraska during the late afternoon and evening hours.  These storms produced hail, strong winds and a few tornadoes.  The storms started out in Gosper county and moved northeast.  There were several reports of funnel clouds in eastern Gosper county.  As the storms continued into Dawson and Buffalo counties, storm chasers continued to report funnels and hail.  The first tornado set down in southeast Sherman county near Rockville and was observed for a few minutes.  The next twister was in southwest Howard county near Farwell.  Another tornado was reported by a sheriff's deputy near the junction of Nebraska highways 92 and 11.  These tornadoes were in open county and caused little in the way of damage.  The town of Elba in Howard county has pummeled by large hail for period of time.  Broken windows and trees stripped of leaves were common throughout the small community.  The most destructive tornado occurred near the village of Cushing.  The tornado set down on a farm west of town and damaged several outbuildings and threw irrigation pipe across a field.  The tornado then cut a path across the south end of town, again damaging several outbuildings and some minor damage to a home.  The tornado dissipated just southeast of town.  Other storms south of these produced strong winds from Grand Island to near Hebron.  Trees and power poles were damaged from the strong winds.

Two rounds of severe thunderstorms pounded south-central Nebraska this Saturday.  Crop damage and flooding were common east of U.S. Highway 281 and south of Interstate 80.  The first round began in the morning.  Heavy rain and hail pounded areas from Superior to north of York and Osceola.  Fillmore county was hardest hit with golf ball size hail and one to two inches of rain.The second round by mid-afternoon targeted the sam area primarily east of Hastings.  Fillmore county took the brunt of the storm with 2 to 5 inches of rain and a swath of severe crop damage from west of Milligan to the north of town.  Large hail and heavy rain was also reported at Shickley.  At the time, Fillmore county was busy with activities.  Geneva was having a softball tournament, while Shickley and Milligan were both having town celebrations.In Sutton, 3 to 4 inches of rain fell in a couple of hours.  The School Creek in northeast Clay county spilled over its banks onto U.S. Highway 6.  A bridge was washed out just west of Sutton, and the creek spilled out of its banks in town.  Large hail was also reported in Clay county.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Visibilities were reduced to a quarter of a mile at times due to blowing dust.
2 inch diamter hail broke windows from 3 to 7 miles northeast of Alliance, Ne.
Hail up to 1 inch in diameter accumulated 2 inches deep.
Dime size hail fell at the NWS office in Valley.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large thunderstorm rolled through the Omaha area producing wind gusts of around 60 to 70 mph.  The storm also dropped some hail, mostly smaller than dime size over the city, but slightly larger west and south of town.  Scattered reports of trees downed were received from across the city and over 8000 customers lost power.  The winds also knocked down a few street lights in town, and a skybox set up at a west Omaha golf course for an upcoming tournament was destroyed.  The strong winds spread from northwest to southeast across the city, lasting around 40 minutes.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Broken winds on house and vehicle.

Picnic table blown around with glass top broken.
Hail drifted 1-2 feet deep along state highway 71 south of Kimball, NE.
Wind gusts estimated from 70-80 mph blew down approximately 40 feet of a 120 foot smokestack at a waste management facility.
Wind driven hail up to nickel size stripped leaves from crops in the area.

Thunderstorm wind gusts estimated at over 60 mph downed many large cottonwood trees in the Big Island area along the Platte River south of Fremont.  Some of the trees fell on mobile homes, vehicles and recreational vehicles causing extensive damage.  A semitrailer truck was overturned at the intersection of Highways 77 and 30 in town slightly injuring 2 people.
Thunderstorm wind gusts were estimated at around 75 mph by law enforcement between Mead and Wahoo.
An amatuer radio operator estimated thunderstorm wind gusts of 75 mph just south of Leshara and law enforcement reported that 7 or 8 large trees were downed west of town due to the winds.
A thunderstorm wind gust was measured at 58 mph at the NWS office in Valley.
Golfball size hail was reported west of Leshara.
Hail estimated at 1 inch in diameter stripped corn fields west of town.
Hail covered the ground.
Microburst wind gusts damaged trailers in mobile home park.
Trees blown down.

Heavy snow and gusty winds occurred over much of the southern half of the Nebraska panhandle with snowfall amounts of 6 to 10 inches common.  The heaviest snow fell in the Scottsbluff, Ne. area where amounts from 9 to 12 inches were reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A winter storm brought the first significant snowfall of the season on this Sunday.  Snowfalls of 6 inches or more were noted generally along and north of the Platte River.  Ashton received the most snow with 13 inches with Osceola close behind with 10 inches.  The area from Fullerton to St. Paul to Cozad had about 8 inches of snow.  Interstate 80 was closed for a few hours from Kearney west during the storm and many schools were closed or delayed on Monday morning.

This was the first winter storm of the 2004/2005 season to hit eastern Nebraska.  Accumulating snow spread into the area around noon Sunday, 11/28/04, and continued into the early morning hours Monday before tapering to flurries or very light snow.  Although little wind accompanied the snow, around 6 to 9 inches was reported from around Wahoo and David City northwest to near Norfolk and Albion.  A few of the heavier amounts included 9 inches in David City, 8 inches at Weston and 7 inches in Columbus.

Thunderstorm wind gusts estimated at over 60 mph uprooted several trees and caused serious damage to many others.  At least 20 to 30 mature trees in town came down or had to be removed due to serious damage.  Fallen tree branches also downed power lines in Lincoln.
Thunderstorm wind gusts estimated at 60 mph blew down some fences in the Millard area around 128th and Harrison Streets.
Thunderstorm wind gusts estimated at over 60 mph blew 4 air-conditioning units off of a local restaurant. The winds also caused some crop damage around town.
Hail up to golfball size was reported south of Cook. The hail caused damage to several cars in the area.
Hail around 1 inch in diameter damaged some cars and roofs of houses and shreaded leaves off of trees and crops from near Fairbury to 5 miles south of town.

Penny size hail was reported by the public near 15th and Center Streets.  The hail was accompanied by winds at least 40 mph and there were several calls made to the City of Omaha works department about tree limbs downed.  Power was knocked out to around 1200 OPPD customers, mainly across the central sections of town.
Hail up to 1 inch in diameter was reported in Steele City for at least 5 minutes.

Thunderstorm wind gusts estimated at 70 mph destroyed a shed and two turkey barns and killed almost 2000 turkeys just south of Waverly.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm wind gusts estimated at over 60 mph downed trees and did some minor roof damage from North Bend to a few miles east of town.  Power was knocked out across parts of North Bend for almost an hour.
Hail up to 1 inch in diameter was reported from Crofton to 4 miles north of town.

A severe thunderstorm that intensified over the Hartington area downed trees and caused other tree damage from Hartington to several miles east of town.  Winds were estimated to be at least 70 mph.  Very heavy rain accompanied the storm which caused some street flooding in town.
Law enforcement reported that thunderstorm wind gusts estimated at around 60 mph downed several tree limbs in Wisner.
Hail up to 1.25 inches in diameter damaged crops around Albion.  Other crop damage was reported near Cedar Rapids and Loretto.  The hail was driven by gusty winds.
Hail, mostly dime size, was driven by strong winds causing a path of crop damage from just north through east of Plainview.  Some damaged crops were also observed south of town.

A brief tornado was observed by a trained spotter in far southwestern Knox county. Before the touchdown, the storm produced several funnel cloud reports as it tracked south along the Holt and Knox county line. The tornado did little or no apparent damage.

Thunderstorm wind gusts estimated at 60 mph downed large tree branches in town.  The winds were accompanied by heavy rain, some locations in town picked up 3 inches, which caused some street flooding.
Several reports of dime size hail was reported mainly across the western parts of town.
Thunderstorm winds downed a swath of trees in Plattsmouth. A pilot reported that wind equipment at the local airport measured the wind gusts at 70 mph.

Thunderstorm wind gusts estimated at over 60 mph blew a semi truck over near Nebraska City. The winds could have been the rear flank downdraft of a developing tornado that eventually touched down about 20 miles to the east.

Thunderstorm wind gusts estimated at over 70 mph downed numerous large trees and power lines in town.  Many homes and cars sustained damage when branches fell on them, and power was knocked out to parts of the town for up to 24 hours.  Several people narrowly escaped serious injury by avoiding falling trees, one in a car and another in a house.
Mostly 1/2 to 1 inch diameter hail.
1/2 to 1 inch diameter hail reported.
Dime to nickel size hail fell in Falls City for a little over 5 minutes.
A NWS Cooperative Observer reported that thunderstorm wind gusts estimated at least 60 mph downed some 6 inch diameter tree limbs in town.
Thunderstorm wind gusts estimated at around 60 mph downed a utility pole in town.
A thunderstorm wind gust of 58 mph was measured by the Tekamah Asos.
A thunderstorm wind gust of 58 mph was measured by ASOS at the Norfolk airport.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong upper level low pressure system that moved from northern Colorado Saturday evening into central Kansas Sunday brought heavy snow, up to 13 inches in some locations, and gusty northwest winds of 20 to 25 mph to Western Nebraska on the 28th.  Snow accumulations began around 5 AM CST on the 28th with the heaviest snow concentrated in an east-west band through Deuel, Garden, Keith, Arthur, McPherson, Lincoln, Logan, and Custer Counties where 10 to 13 inches accumulated.  Because of low visibilities due to heavy snow combined with drifting and numerous vehicles stuck on the roadways, Interstate 80 and Highway 30 were closed from 4 PM CST to 10 PM CST.  Icy roadways contributed to numerous accidents and indirect injuries.

Snow accumulations began around 5 AM CST on the 28th with the heaviest snow concentrated in an east-west band through Deuel, Garden, Keith, Arthur, McPherson, Lincoln, Logan, and Custer Counties where 10 to 13 inches accumulated.  Because of low visibilities due to heavy snow combined with drifting and numerous vehicles stuck on the roadways, Interstate 80 and Highway 30 were closed from 4 PM CST to 10 PM CST.  Icy roadways contributed to numerous accidents and indirect injuries.
